title: [Feature-18070][Task] Add Amazon EMR Serverless task plugin
body:
## Was this PR generated or assisted by AI?
YES. The implementation was assisted by AI (Claude) for code generation, with human review, testing and verification on a real AWS EMR Serverless environment.
## Purpose of the pull request
Add a new task plugin for Amazon EMR Serverless, enabling users to submit, monitor, and cancel Spark/Hive jobs on EMR Serverless applications directly from DolphinScheduler workflows.
Unlike the existing EMR on EC2 task plugin which manages EC2-based clusters, EMR Serverless is a serverless runtime that requires no cluster infrastructure management and automatically scales compute resources on demand.
Close [18070](https://github.com/apache/dolphinscheduler/issues/18070)
## Brief change log
**Backend (new module: `dolphinscheduler-task-emr-serverless`)**
- `EmrServerlessTask` — extends `AbstractRemoteTask`, implements submit/track/cancel lifecycle via AWS SDK v1 (`StartJobRun`, `GetJobRun`, `CancelJobRun`)
- `EmrServerlessParameters` — task parameter model (applicationId, executionRoleArn, jobName, startJobRunRequestJson)
- `EmrServerlessTaskChannel` / `EmrServerlessTaskChannelFactory` — SPI registration via `@AutoService`, registered as `EMR_SERVERLESS`
- `EmrServerlessTaskException` — dedicated exception class
- Authentication: reuses `aws.emr.*` config from `aws.yaml`, falls back to `DefaultAWSCredentialsProviderChain`
- Supports failover recovery via `appIds` (jobRunId)
**Frontend**
- `use-emr-serverless.ts` (fields) — form fields for Application Id, Execution Role Arn, Job Name, StartJobRunRequest JSON editor
- `use-emr-serverless.ts` (tasks) — task model definition
- Registered in task type constants, store, format-data, i18n (en_US/zh_CN)
- Task icon (reuses EMR icon)
**Documentation**
- Chinese doc: `docs/docs/zh/guide/task/emr-serverless.md`
- English doc: `docs/docs/en/guide/task/emr-serverless.md`
- Includes: overview, task parameters, Spark/Hive JSON examples, AWS auth config, job state transitions, screenshots
## Verify this pull request
This change added tests and can be verified as follows:
- Added `EmrServerlessTaskTest` with 11 unit tests covering: success/failed/cancelled lifecycle, full state chain, submit error handling, null GetJobRun response, cancel with/without jobRunId, failover recovery, parameter validation, and invalid JSON handling.
- Manually verified by deploying to an EC2 instance in Standalone mode and successfully submitting a Spark job to a real AWS EMR Serverless application.
author: norrishuang

linked_issues.title: [Feature][Task] Support Amazon EMR Serverless task plugin
linked_issues.body:
### Search before asking

- [x] I had searched in the [issues](https://github.com/apache/dolphinscheduler/issues?q=is%3Aissue) and found no similar feature requirement.


### Description

DolphinScheduler currently supports Amazon EMR on EC2 task type for managing EC2-based clusters and running computing tasks. However, there is no support for [Amazon EMR Serverless](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/emr/latest/EMR-Serverless-UserGuide/emr-serverless.html), which is a serverless deployment option that allows users to run Spark and Hive workloads without managing cluster infrastructure.
EMR Serverless automatically provisions and scales compute resources on demand, offering a simpler operational model and cost optimization through pay-per-use pricing. It has become the recommended way to run EMR workloads for many use cases.
This feature request proposes adding a new `EMR_SERVERLESS` task type that enables users to:
1. **Submit jobs** — Submit Spark or Hive jobs to a pre-created EMR Serverless application via the [StartJobRun API](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/emr-serverless/latest/APIReference/API_StartJobRun.html)
2. **Monitor job status** — Automatically poll job state (SUBMITTED → PENDING → SCHEDULED → RUNNING → SUCCESS/FAILED/CANCELLED) via the [GetJobRun API](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/emr-serverless/latest/APIReference/API_GetJobRun.html)
3. **Cancel jobs** — Automatically cancel running jobs when a DolphinScheduler task is killed, via the [CancelJobRun API](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/emr-serverless/latest/APIReference/API_CancelJobRun.html)
4. **Failover recovery** — Recover tracking of running jobs when Worker nodes restart
### Task Parameters
| Parameter | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| Application Id | EMR Serverless application ID (e.g. `00fkht2eodujab09`) |
| Execution Role Arn | IAM role ARN for job execution |
| Job Name | Optional job name for identification |
| StartJobRunRequest JSON | JSON containing `JobDriver` and `ConfigurationOverrides`

title: [Improvement-18019][task-sql] Support SQL from resource file and parameter placeholders
body:
<!--Thanks very much for contributing to Apache DolphinScheduler, we are happy that you want to help us improve DolphinScheduler! -->

## Was this PR generated or assisted by AI?
YES.  
The implementation and refactoring were done by me, with AI assistance mainly for:
- Designing the overall approach (backend + UI + tests) based on existing patterns (e.g. HiveCli, FlinkSqlGateway, PR #17987 review).
- Drafting some code snippets and PR/issue descriptions, which I reviewed and adapted to fit DolphinScheduler’s style.

## Purpose of the pull request

[Improvement][task-sql] Support executing SQL from **Resource Center files** as well as inline scripts, and apply **parameter placeholders** (`${...}`, `$[...]`) consistently for SQL tasks.

This makes it easier to manage SQL scripts (version control, reuse across workflows) and aligns `task-sql` with other resource-aware task types.

close #18019

## Brief change log

### Backend

- **SqlParameters** (task-api)
  - Add fields:
    - `sqlSource`: `"SCRIPT"` (default) or `"FILE"`
    - `sqlResource`: resource path in Resource Center
  - Keep existing `sql` for inline SQL (backward compatible).
  - Update:
    - `checkParameters()` to accept either `sql` **or** `sqlResource`
    - `getResourceFilesList()` to return a `ResourceInfo` when `sqlResource` is set, so Worker downloads the file.

- **SqlTask** (task-sql)
  - Add `ensureSqlContent()`:
    - If `sql` is empty and `sqlResource` is set:
      - Use `TaskExecutionContext.getResourceContext()` to resolve local file path.
      - Read SQL text from the downloaded resource file and set it into `SqlParameters.sql`.
  - Keep the existing split/bind/execute logic unchanged.
  - Improve title placeholder replacement:
    - Build a placeholder map even when `prepareParamsMap` is `null` (fall back to `Collections.emptyMap()`).
    - Apply `ParameterUtils.convertParameterPlaceholders` for `title` so time placeholders (e.g. `$[yyyyMMdd]`) still work even without custom params.

- **Tests**
  - Add `SqlTaskTest`:
    - `testSqlLoadedFromResourceFileWhenSqlIsEmpty`:
      - Creates a temp SQL file.
      - Configures `sqlResource` to point to it while leaving `sql` empty.
      - Uses `ResourceContext` on `TaskExecutionContext`.
      - Verifies `SqlTask` loads the content into `SqlParameters.sql`.

### Frontend (UI)

- **Task form for SQL task**
  - `use-sql.ts`:
    - Extend `model` with:
      - `sqlSource`: `'SCRIPT' | 'FILE'` (default `'SCRIPT'`)
      - `sqlResource`: selected resource path.
  - `fields/use-sql.ts`:
    - Add a **SQL Source** radio group:
      - `SCRIPT`: show the existing SQL editor (`field: 'sql'`).
      - `FILE`: hide the editor and show a Resource Center file selector bound to `sqlResource` (single-select).
    - Keep original validation for `sql` when `sqlSource === 'SCRIPT'`.

- **Localization**
  - Add i18n keys in `en_US` and `zh_CN`:
    - `sql_source`, `sql_source_script`, `sql_source_file`, `sql_resource_file`.

## Verify this pull request

This change added tests and can be verified as follows:

- **Unit tests**
  - `SqlParametersTest`:
    - Verifies `getResourceFilesList()` returns a non-empty list when `sqlResource` is set.
  - `SqlTaskTest`:
    - `testSqlLoadedFromResourceFileWhenSqlIsEmpty`: verifies `SqlTask` loads SQL content from a Resource Center file via `ResourceContext` when `sql` is empty and `sqlResource` is set.

- **Manual verification**
  - Build backend and UI on `dev` with this branch.
  - In UI:
    - Create a SQL task with:
      - `SQL Source = SCRIPT`, edit inline SQL as before → task behavior unchanged.
      - `SQL Source = FILE`, select a `.sql` file from Resource Center → task executes the SQL from that file.
    - Use `${...}` and `$[...]` placeholders in both modes to confirm they are resolved as expected.

## Pull Request Notice

[Pull Request Notice](https://github.com/apache/dolphinscheduler/blob/dev/docs/docs/en/contribute/join/pull-request.md)

This PR does **not** introduce incompatible changes to existing SQL tasks:
- Existing tasks without `sqlSource/sqlResource` continue to run as before (treated as `SCRIPT` + `sql`).
- New fields are backward compatible and optional.

closes #18019
author: macdoor

linked_issues.title: [Improvement][task-sql] Support executing SQL from resource center file
linked_issues.body:
### Search before asking

- [x] I had searched in the [issues](https://github.com/apache/dolphinscheduler/issues?q=is%3Aissue) and found no similar feature requirement.


### Description

## Purpose

Using Resource Center SQL files makes it much easier to manage SQL scripts:

- SQL scripts can be version-controlled and reviewed independently (e.g. via Git)
- Multiple workflows can share the same SQL file, avoiding duplication
- Updating SQL logic only requires updating the file, without editing every SQL task node

Extend the **SQL** task type to support:

1. Executing SQL from a **Resource Center file** (in addition to inline script)
2. Applying **parameter placeholders** (`${...}`, `$[...]`) consistently for both inline SQL and file-based SQL

This is similar to what other task types already support (e.g. HiveCli, Shell, Spark, Python, Flink/FlinkSqlGateway).

## Background

In current `dev`:

- `SqlParameters` only has a single `sql` field
- The SQL task UI only supports inline SQL text
- Users who keep large SQL scripts in the **Resource Center** cannot reference them directly from the SQL task
- Parameter placeholder replacement is partially handled when binding parameters, but there is no way to:
  - load SQL content from a file first
  - then apply placeholders on that content

For large / shared SQL scripts, putting them into the Resource Center and referencing them from tasks is a common pattern. Tasks like **HiveCli**, **Shell**, **Spark**, **Python** already support this mode.

## Proposal

### Backend

1. **Extend `SqlParameters`** (`dolphinscheduler-task-api`):

   - Add fields:
     - `sqlSource`: `"SCRIPT"` (default) or `"FILE"`
     - `sqlResource`: resource path in Resource Center
   - Keep existing `sql` for inline SQL (backward compatible).
   - Update:
     - `checkParameters()` to accept either `sql` OR `sqlResource`
     - `getResourceFilesList()` to return a `ResourceInfo` when `sqlResource` is set, so Worker downloads the file

2. **Extend `S

body:
Fixes #4743

Summary: Guard the evaluator list's model badge renderer against empty or malformed `modelConfig` values so partially configured evaluators render as `-` instead of crashing the whole page with `"undefined" is not valid JSON`.

Validation: `npm run build:flow` and `npm run build:app` both passed in `spring-ai-alibaba-admin/frontend`. The evaluator list now safely treats missing or invalid `modelConfig` as an unconfigured evaluator state.
author: Gujiassh

title: chore(api-token): hardening fixes
body:
# API token: hardening fixes

Follow-ups to the upstream API-tokens feature (#185) addressing atomicity,
spec-compliance, and a couple of small correctness bugs found while reviewing
the save/auth/revocation paths.

## Changes

- **`RedisApiTokenRepository.save` — MULTI/EXEC instead of pipeline.**
  The hash key, id key, and principal ZSET now commit together. An aborted
  EXEC (returns `null`) is surfaced as an exception instead of silently
  succeeding, so a partial state can't leave a readable hash key with no id
  record.

- **Self-healing name reservation.**
  The `SET NX` on the name index key now carries a 60s TTL. If the JVM dies
  or the connection drops between the reservation and the MULTI commit (or
  the explicit cleanup itself fails), the orphan reservation is auto-evicted
  and the principal can retry the same name. On a successful commit the TTL
  is replaced with the token's real lifetime — `EXPIREAT` for expiring
  tokens, `PERSIST` for non-expiring ones (otherwise a non-expiring token's
  name would free up after 60s).

- **RFC 7235-compliant `Bearer` matching.**
  Both `ApiTokenRequestMatcher` and `ApiTokenAuthenticationFilter` now match
  the `Bearer` auth-scheme case-insensitively (`bearer …` / `BEARER …` are
  valid per RFC 7235 §2.1). The opaque `spk_…` token prefix stays
  case-sensitive.

- **`MDC.remove` locale fix.**
  Use `Locale.ROOT` for the `toUpperCase` of the header name so the MDC key
  is computed deterministically regardless of the JVM's default locale
  (avoids the Turkish-locale `i → İ` class of bugs that would prevent
  scrubbing the plaintext token from MDC).

- **`RedisApiTokenRepository.delete` — surface aborted EXEC.**
  A `null`/empty result from `tx.exec()` (WATCH conflict, connection drop)
  now throws. Without this the token would remain authenticatable until its
  next natural expiry — which for non-expiring tokens is never. Surfacing
  the failure lets the controller return 5xx and the caller retry.

## Test plan

Unit tests added/extended in `RedisApiTokenRepositoryTest` and
`ApiTokenAuthenticationFilterTest` for:

- atomic save commit + rollback on EXEC failure
- name reservation TTL behavior on success/failure paths
- case-insensitive `Bearer` token extraction
- delete throws on aborted EXEC

Run: `./gradlew :gate:gate-web:test --tests '*ApiToken*'`
author: stevenlandrydd

title: fix(docker): Fixes an issue with RBAC around docker images.
body: Clouddriver POST filters the data on finds.  This leads to performance issues when auth gets enabled - and isn't good even before that for ECS deploys.  Add a UI field to deck on docker selection to filter by account, then changed the API to use the PreAuth to validate BEFORE operations are made on said account.
author: jasonmcintosh

title: feat(api-tokens): add API token authentication support
body:
# Spinnaker API Tokens

Adds first-class, revocable API tokens for authenticating programmatic
requests against Gate. Tokens are minted, listed, and revoked through a new
self-service UI in Deck, persisted in Redis by Gate, and resolved on every
inbound request by a dedicated stateless Spring Security chain.

The feature is **off by default** (`api-tokens.enabled=false`) — turning it on
registers all of the new beans, exposes the `/auth/apiTokens` endpoints, and
surfaces the "API Tokens" entry in the user menu.

## Why

Today, automation that talks to Spinnaker has to either ride a user's SSO
session or run as a service account behind some out-of-band auth (basic auth,
mTLS, IAP-issued JWT, etc.). None of those are revocable per-credential,
scoped to a single integration, or auditable from the UI.

API tokens give us:

- **Per-credential revocation.** Every token is independent; revoking one
  doesn't disturb other automation owned by the same user / SA.
- **Bounded lifetime.** Both user and service-account tokens have admin-tunable
  max lifetimes (90 / 365 days by default); user tokens always expire.
- **IAP-compatible transport.** `X-Spinnaker-Token: spk_…` flows cleanly
  through GCP IAP, which rejects non-JWT `Authorization: Bearer` values.
- **Operator observability.** A new `gate.requests` Spectator counter is
  tagged with `authType`, `principalKind`, `method`, `statusCode`, and
  `status` so we can answer "how much of our traffic is token-auth, and from
  which kind of principal?" without scraping logs.

## Token model

A token is a string of the form `spk_<240 bits of hex>`. Only the SHA-256
hash is persisted (in Redis); the plaintext is returned **once** at mint time
and never recoverable again.

Each token record carries:

| field | notes |
|-------|-------|
| `id` | UUID, stable identifier used by the UI and `DELETE /auth/apiTokens/{id}` |
| `name` | Human-readable; **unique per principal**, max 64 chars, no control chars |
| `principalType` | `USER` or `SERVICE_ACCOUNT` |
| `principalId` | Username for `USER` tokens; SA name for `SERVICE_ACCOUNT` |
| `createdByUserId` | The minting user (admins can mint SA tokens on behalf of an SA) |
| `expiresAt` | ISO-8601; required for `USER`, optional (forever) for `SERVICE_ACCOUNT` |
| `lastUsedAt` | Updated asynchronously off the auth hot path |
| `lastFiatCheckAt` | Throttles the optional "is this principal still in Fiat?" recheck |
| `hashRef` | SHA-256 hex of the plaintext; lets revoke-by-id find the hash key in O(1) |

## How the auth chain works

`api-tokens.enabled=true` registers a dedicated
`WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter` (`ApiTokenAuthConfigurerAdapter`, `@Order(50)`)
whose `RequestMatcher` only matches requests bearing `X-Spinnaker-Token:
spk_…` or `Authorization: Bearer spk_…`. That chain is configured
`STATELESS`, so:

- Token auth **never writes to `HttpSession`** and cannot leak into
  cookie-only requests.
- Non-token requests fall through to the existing browser/OAuth2 chain
  untouched.
- Unauthenticated token requests get a clean **`401`** (via
  `HttpStatusEntryPoint`) instead of a 302 to the SSO login page — what API
  clients actually want.

The `ApiTokenAuthenticationFilter` does, in order:

1. Strip `X-Spinnaker-Token` from MDC and wrap the request so kork's
   `AuthenticatedRequestFilter` can't re-emit it as an outbound header to
   downstream services. **This is a real leak source if you skip it** — kork
   auto-copies every `X-Spinnaker-*` header into MDC and then back into
   outbound calls.
2. SHA-256 the plaintext and look it up in Redis via
   `ApiTokenService.resolveByHash`. Unknown hashes → fall through (the
   request is left unauthenticated; downstream entry points decide what to
   do).
3. Optionally throttle-check Fiat (see below) and reject if the principal
   has been deprovisioned.
4. Read the cached `UserPermission.View` from `FiatPermissionEvaluator` —
   **not** `permissionService.login()`, because that triggers a Fiat
   role-provider re-resolve that returns an empty role set for OIDC users
   (whose roles come from the groups claim via `loginWithRoles`) and would
   silently demote the live session.
5. Build a `User` principal so `@SpinnakerUser` resolves correctly in
   controllers, populate `roles` / `allowedAccounts`, install a
   `PreAuthenticatedAuthenticationToken` on the `SecurityContext`, and
   set request attributes (`gate.isApiToken`, `gate.authType`,
   `gate.principalKind`) that `FiatSessionFilter`, future DPoP work, and the
   metrics filter all key off.
6. Fire-and-forget update `lastUsedAt` on a daemon executor (drained on
   `@PreDestroy`).

`FiatSessionFilter` short-circuits when `gate.isApiToken` is set, so the
stateless token path never invalidates a session it doesn't own.

## Redis layout

`RedisApiTokenRepository` owns all token I/O. Keys are namespaced by
`api-tokens.redis-key-prefix` (default `api-token`) so Gate can share a
Redis instance with other services:

```
{prefix}:hash:{sha256_hex}             STRING  → JSON TokenRecord  (EXPIREAT if token expires)
{prefix}:id:{uuid}                     STRING  → JSON TokenRecord  (EXPIREAT)
{prefix}:principal:{TYPE}:{id}         ZSET    → member UUIDs, score = expiry epoch seconds
                                                 (Long.MAX_VALUE for non-expiring tokens)
{prefix}:name:{TYPE}:{id}:{sha(name)}  STRING  → token UUID         (EXPIREAT)
```

Notable invariants:

- The principal ZSET is self-cleaning: `findByPrincipal` and
  `findAllByPrincipalType` both call `ZREMRANGEBYSCORE … 0 now` before
  reading.
- The name index hashes the user-supplied name (fixed-length suffix) so
  `:` characters in `principalId` can't make different (principal, name)
  pairs collide.
- Per-principal name uniqueness is enforced atomically by `SET NX` on the
  name key inside `save()`. The HTTP layer's `rejectDuplicateName` is just a
  fast-path UX optimisation; the repository's `SET NX` is the source of
  truth (and translates to `409 Conflict` via `DuplicateTokenNameException`).
- Revocation issues all four `DEL`s inside `MULTI/EXEC` so no Gate replica
  can observe a partial state.
- `updateLastUsed` / `updateLastFiatCheck` use `WATCH/MULTI/EXEC` with a
  bounded 3-attempt retry so concurrent writers don't clobber each other's
  sibling fields via stale `GET → mutate → SET` round-trips. TTLs are
  preserved across the rewrite.

## HTTP API (`/auth/apiTokens`)

Mounted on `ApiTokenController`, gated by `@ConditionalOnProperty("api-tokens.enabled")`.

| Method | Path | Who | Notes |
|--------|------|-----|-------|
| `GET`    | `/auth/apiTokens` | any authenticated user | Lists caller's `USER` tokens; admins additionally see all `SERVICE_ACCOUNT` tokens. |
| `POST`   | `/auth/apiTokens` | minting role (USER) or admin (SA) | Creates a token. Returns the plaintext **exactly once** under `token`. |
| `DELETE` | `/auth/apiTokens/{id}` | owner or admin | Atomic revocation. |
| `GET`    | `/auth/apiTokens/admin/users` | admin only | All `USER` tokens across all principals. |
| `GET`    | `/auth/apiTokens/serviceAccounts` | admin only | The subset of Front50 service accounts that are declared as token-eligible. |

`CreateApiTokenRequest` is a record (`name`, `principalType`, `principalId`,
`expiresAt`). `expiresAt` is optional; when omitted, user tokens default to
`maxUserTokenLifetimeDays` and SA tokens default to non-expiring. Names are
trimmed, length-checked, and rejected if they contain ISO control characters
(log-injection / display-spoofing risk once the name is echoed in API
responses and the UI).

## Front50 service account changes

Service-account API tokens may only target identities operators have
explicitly declared as "API-capable" in config:

```yaml
service-accounts:
  - name: deploy-bot
    memberOf:
      - deploy-team
```

`ServiceAccountsInitializer` is a Boot `ApplicationRunner` that **upserts**
each declared SA on every startup — config is authoritative; `memberOf` is
overwritten to match the file. A new
`GET /serviceAccounts/tokenEligible` Front50 endpoint returns the
intersection of the SA store and the configured names; Gate calls it both
to populate the create-SA-token dropdown and to validate the
`principalId` on `POST /auth/apiTokens`.

## Fiat changes

Two small, targeted additions:

1. **`PermissionsRepository.isEmpty()`** with efficient implementations:
   - `RedisPermissionsRepository` → `SCARD allUsersKey`.
   - `SqlPermissionsRepository` → `fetchExists(selectOne().from(USER))`.

   `AuthorizeController.getUserPermission` now returns **`503`** instead of
   `404` when the repository is empty (likely an accidental Redis flush or
   not-yet-warm SQL store), so token auth — and the existing session
   flow — fail open instead of treating every principal as departed.

2. **`PermissionService.getRolesForTokenAuth`** in `gate-core` calls Fiat
   directly (bypassing the local cache) inside `AuthenticatedRequest.allowAnonymous`
   and **propagates** `SpinnakerHttpException` so callers can distinguish a
   legitimate empty-roles response from a transient Fiat failure. This is
   what powers the token filter's optional
   `reject-if-no-principal-permissions` mode without breaking the live
   session of an in-flight user.

## Gate observability: `gate.requests`

`RequestMetricsFilter` (registered at `LOWEST_PRECEDENCE - 2`) emits one
Spectator counter per inbound HTTP request:

```
name: gate.requests
tags:
  authType:      api_token | session | oauth2 | x509 | pre_authenticated | anonymous | none
  principalKind: user | service_account | unknown
  method:        GET | POST | PUT | DELETE | …
  statusCode:    200 | 401 | 503 | …
  status:        2xx | 4xx | 5xx | …
```

`AuthTypeResolver` centralises classification: filters that know better
publish to request attributes (`gate.authType`, `gate.principalKind`);
otherwise we fall back to heuristics over `SecurityContextHolder`.
High-cardinality data (principal id, URI, query) is **deliberately not**
tagged.

## `GET /auth/user` change

The previous implementation returned a reflection-derived
`Map<String,Object>` over the Spring Security `User`, which silently
exposed every new field. It's been replaced with an explicit
`AuthUserResponse` record. The wire format is byte-for-byte compatible with
the legacy map for existing consumers, plus:

- `isAdmin: boolean`
- `canMintApiTokens: boolean`
- `maxUserTokenLifetimeDays: number?` (omitted when `api-tokens.enabled=false`)
- `maxServiceAccountTokenLifetimeDays: number?` (same)

`ApiTokenService.canMintApiTokens` is the single source of truth used by
both the UI flag (`AuthController.user`) and the POST enforcement path
(`ApiTokenController.create`) so the two can't drift. Resolution order:

1. Subsystem disabled → no.
2. Fiat admin → yes (bypass).
3. No `allowed-minting-roles` configured → no (for non-admins).
4. Otherwise the user must hold at least one configured minting role.

## Deck UI

A new `apitoken/` package under `packages/core/src`:

- **`ApiTokensPageContainer`** — fetches `/auth/user`, then renders…
- **`ApiTokensPage`** — sections for **My Tokens**, **Service Account
  Tokens** (admin), and **All User Tokens** (admin). Tables show name,
  expiry (red when past, "Never" for non-expiring), last-used, and a
  Revoke button.
- **`CreateApiTokenModal`** — name input with live max-length / duplicate
  detection, SA dropdown (admins, populated from
  `/auth/apiTokens/serviceAccounts`), expiry picker capped at the server's
  configured max. The "Maximum (N days, set by server policy)" radio
  intentionally surfaces that the cap is policy, not UI.
- **`RevokeApiTokenButton`** — confirmation modal; surfaces error state on
  network failure.
- **`ApiTokensPage.less`** — page-scoped styles, no global leakage.

## Configuration reference

```yaml
api-tokens:
  enabled: false
  token-prefix: spk_
  redis-key-prefix: api-token
  reject-check-interval-seconds: 60
  max-user-token-lifetime-days: 90
  max-service-account-token-lifetime-days: 365
  reject-if-no-principal-permissions: false
  allowed-minting-roles:
    - spinnaker-api-users
```

`token-random-bytes` (default 30 → 240 bits of entropy) is also tunable but
not commonly changed. Only affects newly minted tokens.

## Security notes

- **No plaintext is ever logged or persisted.** Only the SHA-256 hex hash
  is written to Redis, and the token's auth-time path
  (`extractToken → sha256Hex → findByHash`) never logs the plaintext.
- **`X-Spinnaker-Token` is stripped from MDC and from the wrapped request**
  before the filter chain continues, defending against kork's
  MDC → outbound-header propagation.
- **Per-principal name uniqueness is enforced server-side via `SET NX`**,
  not on the client. Concurrent creates with the same name resolve to
  `409 Conflict`.
- **Revocation is atomic across all four Redis keys** (hash, id, ZSET,
  name) inside `MULTI/EXEC`. There is no window where the hash key (the
  only key read on the auth hot path) outlives the rest.
- **Names are rejected if they contain ISO control characters** (`\0`,
  `\t`, `\n`, `\r`, ANSI escapes, …) — log-injection and display-spoofing
  risk once names are echoed in API responses and the UI.
- **Service-account principals are validated against the Front50
  token-eligible list** at create time, so admins can't mint tokens for
  arbitrary SAs that happen to exist in Front50.

## Operability

- `api-tokens.enabled=false` is the default. Flipping the flag is the only
  thing needed to roll the feature out; rolling it back leaves the Redis
  keys in place (they'll expire on their own).
- Fiat fail-open: if Fiat is unreachable on a token-auth request, the
  filter accepts the cached principal and logs a warning. Only an explicit
  `404` (principal definitively absent) — or a `503` once the repo is
  empty — causes a rejection.
- `lastUsedAt` and `lastFiatCheckAt` updates are best-effort; the auth hot
  path never blocks on them. The async pool is a single daemon thread,
  drained on `@PreDestroy` with a 2 s timeout.
- Admin-list endpoints (`/admin/users`, `/serviceAccounts`) use `SCAN`
  rather than `KEYS`. SCAN can return duplicates across iterations, so we
  dedupe principal keys via a `LinkedHashSet`.

### Manual verification before merge

- [x] Enable `api-tokens.enabled=true` in a dev Gate, log in, mint a user
      token, hit a Gate endpoint with `X-Spinnaker-Token`.
- [x] Mint the same token name twice → second request returns `409`.
- [x] Revoke a token, retry → `401` (or fall-through, depending on
      downstream entry point).
- [x] As admin, mint an SA token for a configured SA, list it in the
      Service Account Tokens table.
- [x] Confirm `gate.requests` counter has both `authType=api_token` and
      `authType=session` after a mixed-traffic burst.
- [x] Confirm `X-Spinnaker-Token` does **not** appear in downstream service
      logs (Fiat, Orca) after a token request.
author: stevenlandrydd

title: feat(provider/google, deck/google): Add certificateMap support for GCE HTTP(S) load balancers
body:
# feat(provider/google, deck/google): Add certificateMap support for GCE HTTP(S) load balancers

## Summary

Add Certificate Manager `certificateMap` support for Google HTTP(S) load balancers alongside legacy `certificate` behavior. Includes null-safe `sslCertificates` extraction across all affected caching and upsert paths, global `certificateMap` write support via `targetHttpsProxies.setCertificateMap`, Deck name-based input UX with cert-source XOR handling, and deterministic `certificate` <-> `certificateMap` migration paths.

## Changes

| Area | Details |
|------|---------| 
| Provider hardening | Null-safe `sslCertificates` extraction across caching and upsert paths (HTTP, INTERNAL_MANAGED, SSL); regional cert URL fix for internal HTTP LB target proxy create + compare |
| Provider feature | `certificateMap` fields on LB model/description; `certificate` XOR `certificateMap` validation for HTTP flows; GCP resource-name regex validation for `certificateMap` (matches Deck contract); rejection for INTERNAL_MANAGED and SSL; `setCertificateMap` write path for global HTTPS proxies; map-to-cert migration clears stale map so `sslCertificates` becomes authoritative again; `getCertificateMapName` trims whitespace and treats whitespace-only input as absent |
| Deck feature | Optional `certificateMap` on domain/template/listener types; cert-source radio UX (global HTTP only); name-based input with server-side URL construction; serializer/deserializer backward compat for listeners without explicit cert source; HTTPS type inference for map-only listeners; listener form split into two rows to prevent Bootstrap column overflow when cert-source radios are visible |

### Files Added

* `GoogleLoadBalancerCertificateExtractionSpec.groovy` — regression coverage for null/empty `sslCertificates` across all three caching agents; compound certificateMap + null sslCerts scenario
* `listener.component.spec.js` — XOR source switching, INTERNAL_MANAGED forced mode, port-change clearing, URL-to-name normalization
* `transformer.service.spec.js` — cert-only and map-only serialization, deserialization with URL normalization, serialize fallback without explicit `certificateSource`, cert-only and empty deserialize
* `loadBalancerType.component.spec.js` — HTTPS detection for certificate, certificateMap, neither, both present simultaneously, and non-HTTP type passthrough

### Files Modified

* `GoogleHttpLoadBalancerCachingAgent.groovy` — null-safe cert extraction, `certificateMap` read from target proxy
* `GoogleInternalHttpLoadBalancerCachingAgent.java` — null-safe cert extraction, `certificateMap` read from target proxy
* `GoogleSslLoadBalancerCachingAgent.groovy` — null-safe cert extraction
* `UpsertGoogleHttpLoadBalancerAtomicOperation.groovy` — `setCertificateMap` write path, stale map clear on cert fallback
* `UpsertGoogleInternalHttpLoadBalancerAtomicOperation.java` — regional cert URL fix, null-safe extraction
* `UpsertGoogleSslLoadBalancerAtomicOperation.groovy` — null-safe cert extraction
* `GoogleHttpLoadBalancer.groovy` / `GoogleInternalHttpLoadBalancer.java` — `certificateMap` model field + view
* `UpsertGoogleLoadBalancerDescription.groovy` — `certificateMap` description field
* `UpsertGoogleLoadBalancerDescriptionValidator.groovy` — XOR validation, INTERNAL_MANAGED and SSL rejection
* `GCEUtil.groovy` — `buildCertificateMapUrl`, `CERTIFICATE_MANAGER_API_PREFIX`
* `listener.component.js` / `listener.component.html` — cert-source radio UX, name input, XOR state handlers
* `transformer.service.js` — serialize/deserialize with map-name normalization and backward compat
* `loadBalancerType.component.js` — HTTPS inference for `certificateMap`-only listeners
* `loadBalancer.ts` / `templates.ts` / `loadBalancer.setTransformer.ts` — type definitions and wiring
* `gce.help.ts` — `certificateMap` help text
* `UpsertGoogleHttpLoadBalancerAtomicOperationUnitSpec.groovy` — certificateMap upsert and migration tests
* `UpsertGoogleInternalHttpLoadBalancerAtomicOperationUnitSpec.groovy` — null-safety tests
* `UpsertGoogleSslLoadBalancerAtomicOperationUnitSpec.groovy` — null-safety tests
* `UpsertGoogleLoadBalancerDescriptionValidatorSpec.groovy` — XOR and rejection tests
* `GCEUtilSpec.groovy` — `buildCertificateMapUrl` URL construction test

## Backward Compatibility

Legacy `certificate` path is fully preserved. Services without `certificateMap` are unaffected — all new fields are nullable, the Deck serializer infers cert source for pre-existing listeners that lack the explicit `certificateSource` field, and the validator only enforces XOR when both fields are present. The existing certificate dropdown remains the default mode.

## Test Coverage

* Null/empty `sslCertificates` extraction for HTTP, INTERNAL_MANAGED, and SSL caching agents
* `buildCertificateMapUrl` URL construction
* `certificate` XOR `certificateMap` validation (HTTP, INTERNAL_MANAGED, SSL)
* Global upsert: create with cert, create with map, update cert-to-map, update map-to-cert with stale map clear
* Operate-level cert-to-map migration: asserts `setCertificateMap` with correct URL, `setSslCertificates` not called
* Operate-level map-to-map rotation: asserts `setCertificateMap` with new URL, no clear
* Operate-level cert rotation (no prior map): asserts only `setSslCertificates`, no `setCertificateMap`
* `getCertificateMapName` whitespace/null/empty/padded normalization
* Validator regex rejection for invalid map names (`""`, `"  "`, `"1map"`, `"-map"`, `"map-"`, `"MAP"`, `"map_name"`, `"map.name"`)
* Validator acceptance of full Certificate Manager URLs
* Deck listener XOR switching, INTERNAL_MANAGED forced source, port-change field clearing
* Deck serializer cert-only and map-only round-trips
* Deck deserializer URL-to-name normalization and `certificateSource` inference
* HTTPS type inference for `certificate`, `certificateMap`, neither, both present simultaneously, and non-HTTP type passthrough
* Transformer serialize fallback for listeners without explicit `certificateSource`
* Transformer deserialize for cert-only listener and neither-cert-nor-map listener
* Compound caching-agent scenario: certificateMap set with null sslCertificates

## References

1. [TargetHttpsProxy resource](https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/reference/rest/v1/targetHttpsProxies) — `certificateMap` semantics, global support, precedence over certs
2. [setCertificateMap method](https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/reference/rest/v1/targetHttpsProxies/setCertificateMap) — global target HTTPS proxy API
3. [setSslCertificates method](https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/reference/rest/v1/targetHttpsProxies/setSslCertificates) — replace certs semantics
4. [target-https-proxies update](https://cloud.google.com/sdk/gcloud/reference/compute/target-https-proxies/update) — `--clear-certificate-map` CLI surface
5. [Certificate Manager maps](https://cloud.google.com/certificate-manager/docs/maps) — map model and usage
author: mrusan

title: feat(console): let standard agents import and auto-invoke published workflows
body:
## What

Adds a new "Workflow" capability to the standard (instruction-type) Agent: users can import published workflows (own / same-space, up to 30) on the capability page, and during chat/debug the model autonomously invokes each imported workflow as a Spring AI tool, feeding the result back into the conversation.

## How

Mirrors the existing plugin (`tools`) capability wiring end to end:

- **Persistence**: new `chat_bot_base.workflows` JSON column (Flyway `V1.42`), `BotCreateForm.BotWorkflow` DTO, `serializeWorkflows`, explicit `botDetail` column + `BotDetail`/`BotInfoDto` readback.
- **Runtime (toolkit)**: `AgentWorkflowRuntimeService` resolves flow ids into agent-callable definitions (tool name `workflow_<flowId>`, JSON schema from the workflow start-node inputs; file params exposed as URL strings, `array-*` mapped to array schemas) and executes synchronously via `WorkflowChatRunClient` (`stream=false` against `workflow.chatUrl`, 180s timeouts); interrupt (`event_data`) and error frames are mapped to readable tool errors.
- **Runtime (hub)**: `WorkflowToolCallbackFactory` + 5th `workflows` param on `AgentToolCallbackResolver.resolve`, wired through `AgentChatTask`/`SpringAiAgentChatService` and all three chat paths (formal / re-answer / debug); on-shelf bots read back from `ChatBotBase` like the other capability fields.
- **Security**: save-side ownership check (`checkWorkflowsAccessible`: exists, not deleted, own uid or same space) in `BotCreateController` create/update; the same gate enforced on `/chat-message/bot-debug` (client-supplied workflows JSON), with `space-id` header only honored after `SpaceInfoUtil.checkUserBelongSpace()` membership validation (endpoint has no `@SpacePreAuth`).
- **Frontend**: Workflow capability block + `WorkflowSelectModal` (published-only via `listFlows({status:1})`), `normalizeWorkflows` (dedup + cap 30), save/reload wiring incl. the 4 legacy-view inline payloads, `workflows` passthrough on all 6 `PromptTry` instances.

## Testing

- Backend `mvn package`: 1945 tests, 0 failures (new: `AgentWorkflowRuntimeServiceTest` 11 cases incl. schema conversion, ownership matrix, error/interrupt/bad-JSON paths, name-collision/truncation; `WorkflowChatRunClientTest` 3 cases over a real embedded HTTP server; resolver/factory tests).
- Frontend `type-check`/eslint: 0 errors in the six touched files.

## Notes / follow-ups (out of scope)

- The pre-existing `tools` (plugin) debug passthrough has the same unvalidated-ownership gap; recommended as a follow-up.
- Optional hardening: server-side cap of 30 in `serializeWorkflows`.

title: feat: standalone agent Skill capability (read_skill / run_skill)
body:
## What

Adds a **Skill capability to the standalone Agent** (the Java Spring AI chat runtime), in three phases:

1. **Persistence + capability-page UI** — a Skill block on the agent capability page (between Knowledge Base and MCP Server) that reuses the importable-skill list (`GET /skill-file/importable`) with search / multi-select / remove and a 30-item cap. Selected skills are stored as a JSON column `chat_bot_base.skills` (`[{skillId,name,description}]`, Flyway `V1.38`) and rehydrated on edit.
2. **read_skill runtime** — shared `SkillEnrichmentService` (extracted from `WorkflowService`, which now delegates) resolves downloadUrl/resources/sandbox by skillId; `ReadSkillToolCallback` (pure-Java HTTP download of SKILL.md + referenced resources) is registered per skill, and the enriched skills flow through all standalone chat branches (formal / re-answer / debug).
3. **run_skill runtime** — `RunSkillToolCallback` executes a skill command in the E2B sandbox via a new internal `core/agent` endpoint `POST /agent/v1/skill/sandbox-exec` (reuses the audited `E2BSandboxProvider`; E2B has no Java SDK). `api.url.agentUrl` (env `AGENT_URL`) wires console-hub → core-agent. v1 does not collect/upload sandbox artifacts.

## Notes

- Targets `main` (the runtime refactor from #1418 is already here); cherry-picked cleanly onto `main` with zero conflicts.
- The standalone bot has no `plugin` JSON, so skills live on `chat_bot_base.skills` rather than `plugin.skills`.
- `run_skill` only executes when an E2B sandbox is configured in resource management; otherwise it returns the standard "unconfigured" message. `AGENT_URL` defaults to `http://core-agent:${CORE_AGENT_PORT}` in docker.

## Verification

- `mvn -DskipTests package -pl hub -am` → BUILD SUCCESS (test compilation included).
- Frontend `type-check` clean for the touched files.
- Passed an internal code review (no Critical issues; skillId dedup hardening applied).

title: feat: standalone agent Skill capability (read_skill / run_skill)
body:
## Summary

Adds end-to-end **Skill** capability to the standalone (single) agent, delivered in three phases. Skills selected on the capability page are persisted, enriched at runtime, and made callable by the agent as `read_skill` / `run_skill`.

### Phase 1 — Capability-page Skill block + persistence
- New Skill block on the agent capability page with a selection modal.
- Selected skill IDs are persisted on `chat_bot_base.skills`.
- Selected skills are forwarded in the debug (try) request so the debug runtime sees them.

### Phase 2 — `read_skill` runtime
- Shared `SkillEnrichmentService` enriches selected skill IDs into runtime skill definitions.
- Java HTTP download path retrieves skill content for the standalone agent.

### Phase 3 — `run_skill` execution
- New `core/agent` sandbox-exec endpoint executes a skill in an isolated sandbox.
- `AGENT_URL` wired through so console-hub can reach `core/agent`.
- Skill tool factory deduplicates `skillIds` when building the tool callbacks.

## Commits (8)
- `9969cb2f` feat(agent-skills): persist standalone-agent skills
- `63953d97` feat(agent-skills): capability-page Skill block + selection modal
- `7138cf33` feat(agent-skills): read_skill runtime for the standalone agent
- `65651580` feat(agent-skills): forward selected skills in the debug (try) request
- `29b862d6` feat(agent-skills): run_skill via a core/agent sandbox endpoint
- `426b1511` chore(agent-skills): wire AGENT_URL so console-hub reaches core-agent
- `9e890934` fix(agent-skills): dedup skillIds when building skill tool callbacks
- `a14dfcd0` docs: set CLAUDE.md heading to # CLAUDE.md

## Notes
- Targets the **`bugfix/superteam`** integration branch (not `main`).
- Passed an internal code review (no Critical issues).

title: fix: spark tool-call filtering + chat loading-spinner edge cases
body:
## Summary

Three fixes for Spark tool-calls and chat loading-spinner edge cases.

### 1. Spark tool-call filtering
Spark's OpenAI-compatible endpoint streams a spurious empty-name `tool_call`, which made Spring AI's resolver throw `toolName cannot be null or empty` and abort the turn. A `BlankToolCallFilteringToolCallingManager` wrapper now drops blank-name tool calls before execution, so Spark tool calls work (deepseek is unaffected).

### 2. Loading spinner on previous bubble
The spinner appeared on a prior answer bubble when a new question started, because `showLoading` was keyed only on the global `isLoading` flag plus a missing sid. It is now gated on `isLastMessage`.

### 3. Spinner not clearing after stop
`stopAnswer` only called the backend stop API and never cleared the streaming state, so the spinner lingered after the user stopped generation. It now calls `finishStreamingMessage()` to clear the spinner immediately.

## Changed files
- `console/backend/.../service/chat/springai/BlankToolCallFilteringToolCallingManager.java` (new)
- `console/backend/.../service/chat/springai/ChatModelFactory.java`
- `console/frontend/src/pages/chat-page/components/message-list.tsx`
- `console/frontend/src/pages/chat-page/index.tsx`

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

title: feat: improve MySQL compatibility and streamline model provider configuration
body:
## Summary

  This PR consolidates a series of backend, database, workflow, and frontend updates focused on two major areas:

  1. Improving MySQL compatibility across database, backend, workflow, and Docker-related modules
  2. Simplifying and stabilizing model provider selection, configuration, and related UI behavior

  It also includes several follow-up fixes for release content, links, locale wording, SDK deprecation handling, and
  page interaction issues.

  ## Main Changes

  ### MySQL compatibility and infrastructure
  - Updated database project to improve MySQL compatibility
  - Optimized backend and workflow projects for MySQL support
  - Adjusted Docker configuration for the MySQL-related deployment scenario
  - Added logs and applied static code analysis optimizations
  - Improved DML-related database handling

  ### Model provider and model selection improvements
  - Simplified model provider selection to three main vendors
  - Updated model provider enums and related dependencies
  - Refined model selection UI to display specific models in the sidebar
  - Allowed model provider selection in the official model config modal
  - Removed references to deprecated model provider enum values
  - Isolated official model page filters from global state
  - Reverted shared filter hook usage and restored model-specific filtering
  - Restored missing components and logic in the official model page

  ### SDK and integration updates
  - Enhanced readability of Anthropic and Google SDK implementations
  - Updated Google GenAI SDK to handle deprecation-related changes

  ### Additional fixes and cleanup
  - Updated locale text to use `OpenAI` instead of `OpenAI Compatible`
  - Fixed repeated interface call issues
  - Modified release copy
  - Fixed OSS link issues
  - Added WeChat Work QR code
  - Removed unused imports and corrected import statements

  ## Impact

  These changes improve database compatibility for MySQL-based deployments, make model provider configuration clearer
  and more maintainable, and fix several UI and integration issues affecting official model management and external SDK
  usage.

  ## Notes

  This PR includes merged changes from multiple feature and bugfix branches and serves as an aggregated integration
  update.

title: More context for AI tool
body:
### Pull Request Description

This PR extends the means our AI may gather information:
1. Claude get access to stdlib and has tools to parse the code there
2. It may also ask fronted to evaluate expression in the current function and give the result.

The extended capabilities are tested by two special package tests simulating user soliving some of the prep challenges. One is meant to test stdlib reading (the data context is provided in prompts, but AI must use some more obscure options available in Table API), and another needs both. The tests were confirmed to fail when the required capability wasn't implemented, so they are good for catching regressions too.

Here is one of the tests in action:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/90965841-fd00-4999-90da-45cb63fe119e

In the web console claude's evaluation requests and our responses are visible.

author: farmaazon

title: Sync visualizations with ydoc-server
body:
### Pull Request Description

<!--
- Please describe the nature of your PR here, as well as the motivation for it.
- If it fixes an open issue, please mention that issue number here.
-->

See also:

- PR #13179

This PR moves visualization requests and responses off the JSON-RPC/binary Language Server protocol and onto a Yjs subdoc synchronized via ydoc-server.

Changelog:
- refactor: move visualization attach/detach/modify/execute-expression from language server JSON-RPC into the ydoc-server                                          
- add: `VisualizationBridgeServer` (Java) and `visualizationBridge.ts `(ydoc) to bridge runtime visualization updates over the binary channel
- add: `app/ydoc-shared/src/visualizations.ts` with shared visualization types and yjsModel sync hooks                                                               
- delete: Attach/Detach/Modify/ExecuteExpression request handlers, `VisualizationApi`, `VisualizationUpdateFactory`, and related JSON-RPC requests                     
- delete: visualization entries from the binary protocol Flatbuffers schema                                                       
- update: GUI `executionContext`, `project`, and `visualizationDataRegistry` providers to consume visualizations via ydoc                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       
- update: `docs/infrastructure/ydoc.md` and trim visualization sections from `protocol-language-server.md`          

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d0612e6d-6f48-4bef-87bf-19066c4a558d

### Performance

The startup time of large project improved from ~3:30 minutes to ~3:00 minutes (**~20%** speedup)

### Important Notes

<!--
- Mention important elements of the design.
- Mention any notable changes to APIs.
-->

Currently the client attaches visualizations by sending `executionContext/attachVisualization` (and `detach` / `modify`) on the JSON channel and receives results as FlatBuffers `VisualizationUpdate` notifications on the binary channel. The binary update carries only `(visualizationId, contextId, expressionId, data)` and there is no pointer back to he request that produced the data, so the client has to guess staleness and compatibility by comparing the current preprocessor to the one in effect when the last request went out (`visualizationConfigPreprocessorEqual` in `executionContext.ts`). @kazcw  flagged this in Discord: we lose information about which inputs produced which output, which makes "stale but still compatible" rendering ad-hoc.

We also already have an authoritative, CRDT-synced representation of the module in ydoc-server (the `DistributedProject` / `ModuleDoc` tree). PR #13179 demonstrated attaching `ExpressionUpdate` data to AST metadata; the consensus in that PR's review was that high-frequency metadata should live in a separate subdoc to avoid bloating the main doc.

The refactor replaces the three JSON-RPC endpoints and the FlatBuffers `VisualizationUpdate` push with a vis subdoc that both sides observe. The client writes a slot per request; ydoc-server notices and calls into the LS via a new
 YjsChannel.Server` callback; the LS drives the runtime and pushes responses back over the channel; ydoc-server writes them into the slot; the client sees them via CRDT sync.

### Why a Yjs subdoc, not the main module doc

The main module doc is already CRDT-heavy with high-value AST edits. Adding potentially megabyte-scale visualization updates to the same doc creates two problems:

1. **GC pressure:** Yjs retains item history for CRDT causality; large bytes under frequently-updated keys accumulate as tombstones.
2. **Starvation:** syncing a 1 MB visualization update would delay AST edits sharing the same WebSocket frame pipeline.

A separate subdoc isolates vis churn, can be loaded lazily, and critically matches the precedent set by PR #13179's review for expression updates. Yjs natively handles subdocs via the y-websocket provider on the client and the `'subdocs'` event on the server, so the infrastructure is already in place.

### Why one subdoc per project, not per module

Visualizations are scoped to an `ExecutionContext`, which today is project-level (clients create contexts against the project's main module, the context manager is per-project). Slots also need to outlive a single module being open, a user might inspect a node in one module while driving execution from another.

A per-module vis subdoc would force us to route slots to the module that owns the expression, which couples vis state to module editing state. A single per-project subdoc is simpler and aligns with how execution contexts already live in the runtime.

### Why the outer map is keyed by request id

This is the single most important design decision, and it came directly from @kazcw feedback.

A visualization-id-keyed map would be a 1:1 translation of today's protocol: attach creates, modify mutates, detach removes. But Keziah pointed out this loses information about staleness:

> If it's keyed by vis id, and the requests can be mutated, it would be a 1:1
> translation of the current protocol, which works, but loses information about
> staleness […]. If it's keyed by request ID and requests are treated as
> immutable, the protocol is more general and we know what inputs produced each
> output.

Keying by a fresh request id (UUID v4) per attach/modify action means:

- Every slot pairs its `request` (immutable preprocessor/args) with its `response` unambiguously - no more "is this data compatible with my current request?" heuristics.
- Modify is naturally expressed as a detach+attach pair on two distinct slots, without mutating the old slot's request.
- Multi-vis per node falls out for free: several slots with distinct `visualizationId`s can share a `nodeExternalId`.

A stale-but-compatible rendering mode during a modify gap (keeping the old response visible while the new slot is pending) is _not_ implemented here as the detach currently deletes the old slot immediately. Adding it cheaply would require holding the old slot until the new one reaches a terminal state. Left as future work.

**Rejected alternatives** and why:

- `visualizationId` as key: loses staleness info; forces mutable requests; can't model multiple in-flight requests for the same visualization id.
- `nodeExternalId` as key: violates the multi-visualization-per-node requirement the Language Server's `ExecutionContextManager` explicitly supports.
- Composite `${visualizationId}:${revision}`: buys nothing over a flat UUID, makes the observer code in the bridge harder (it has to parse and compare).

### Why reuse YjsChannel at all instead of a dedicated interface

Reuse wins because:

- `YjsCallbacksSynchronized` + `YjsChannelSynchronized` + `YdocScheduledExecutorService` already handle the GraalJS single-thread constraint. A new interface means rewriting that scaffolding.
- `InspectManager` already taps JSON and binary channels via `wrapJsonServer` / `wrapBinaryServer`. Reusing the channel abstraction means a trivial `wrapVisControlServer` / `wrapVisDataServer`. A new interface means a second inspect path.
- Y.Array-backed channels give total ordering and `senderId`-based echo suppression for free and semantically what we want for vis messages.

**Trade-off accepted:** the LS decodes JSON strings to typed messages itself (already the case for the JSON-RPC path), rather than receiving pre-parsed Java objects. This is a small amount of boilerplate in `VisualizationBridgeActor` in exchange for everything above.

### Why two YjsChannels, not one

The channel for the bridge has to carry both small JSON control messages (attach/detach/fail - KBs) and potentially large binary response payloads (~1 MB for tables). Options:

1. **One JSON channel**: base64-encode the response bytes into the JSON message. Adds ~33% bandwidth overhead and makes ydoc-inspect harder to read.
2. **One binary channel**: everything as FlatBuffers. Control messages become inscrutable in ydoc-inspect. Flatbuffer schema adds complexity.
3. **Split: `vis:control` (JSON) + `vis:data` (binary)**. Frame binary payloads as `[16-byte requestId][bytes]` so the receiver can correlate. Control stays readable in inspect. Bulk stays on the binary path.

(3) is what the `vis:*` channels do. It also mirrors the existing JSON/binary channel split, so the thread-safety and inspect patterns apply unchanged.

### Extending the slot model to `execute`

`executionContext/executeExpression` was originally kept on JSON-RPC because its one-shot shape (submit expression, receive one result, never subscribe) does not obviously benefit from a CRDT. In practice, keeping it on the legacy path forced the binary `VISUALIZATION_UPDATE` FlatBuffers table, a `VisualizationUpdateFactory`, the `BinaryConnectionController` case, and the oneshot tracking/auto-detach state machine in `ContextEventsListener` to stick around just for this single caller. Migrating it onto the same vis subdoc collapses all of that.

The extension is minimal: a one-shot evaluation is encoded as a regular slot whose `request.expression` is `{ inFrame: <expr> }` as there is no separate `kind` field on `VisualizationSlotSpec`. The bridge detects one-shots via `isInFrameRequest()` on the request shape and treats them as terminal: the slot goes `pending -> ready | failed` and the client deletes it on read; no `detach` message is ever emitted, because the runtime already treats the underlying visualization as short-lived. The bridge reuses the same `[16-byte requestId][bytes]` data framing and the same `ready` / `failed` control messages.

## Invariants to preserve

These are invariants a reviewer should check the implementation maintains, and that future modifications must not break:

1. **Requests are immutable.** Once a slot's `'request'` field is written, it is never updated. Modifying a visualization means creating a new slot under a fresh request id and removing the old one. If you see code that mutates an existing slot's request, that's a bug.
2. **Outer map key - inner `'visualizationId'`.** The outer key is the request id, the inner `'visualizationId'` is the logical visualization id. These are different. The bridge and registry must key state on the outer key, not the inner `'visualizationId'`.
3. **`response` is only written by ydoc-server.** The client reads but never writes response bytes.
4. **Detach is a slot deletion, not a terminal status.** The client's way to tear down an attach is `Visualizations.removeSlot(requestId)`. The bridge observes the deletion and emits a detach message to the LS (unless the slot was an `inFrame` one-shot, in which case removal after response is expected). `VisSlotStatus` has only three values `pending | ready | failed`, and ready/failed are the only terminal statuses that sit in the subdoc. Failed slots remain until the client supersedes or removes them so the registry can surface the error. Do not reintroduce a `'detached'` status as it accumulates stale response bytes in the subdoc for no benefit.
5. **The bridge is the only writer of `'status'` on the server side.** Any LS message that modifies status goes through the bridge. This keeps the CRDT write pattern single-threaded on the ydoc-server side.
6. **Cross-client scoping uses `contextId`, not subdocs.** All clients sharing a project see the same vis subdoc. Clients filter slots by `contextId` they know about. Do not introduce per-client subdocs; that re-creates the multi-session plumbing we are trying to delete.

### Checklist

Please ensure that the following checklist has been satisfied before submitting the PR:

- [x] The documentation has been updated, if necessary.
- [x] Screenshots/screencasts have been attached, if there are any visual changes. For interactive or animated visual changes, a screencast is preferred.
- [x] All code follows the
      [Scala](https://github.com/enso-org/enso/blob/develop/docs/style-guide/scala.md),
      [Java](https://github.com/enso-org/enso/blob/develop/docs/style-guide/java.md),
      [TypeScript](https://github.com/enso-org/enso/blob/develop/docs/style-guide/typescript.md),

title: Support for Chinese Characters
body:

Fixes an issue that if the project is in a folder path in Windows with a UTF-16 character, it fails to launch.

- Use `W` version of the Win32 API for controlling the working folder.

title: Replace language server WebSocket connection with Ydoc
body:
### Pull Request Description

<!--
- Please describe the nature of your PR here, as well as the motivation for it.
- If it fixes an open issue, please mention that issue number here.
-->

- close #14142

PR replaces binary and JSON-RPC WebSockets with Yjs based communication.

```
+------------------+                +--------------------------------------------------------------+
|                  |                |                                                              |
| +--------------+ |    WebSocket   | +-----------------+   YjsChannel.Server  +-----------------+ |
| |  IDE Client  | |<-------------->| |  Ydoc Server    |<-------------------->| Language Server | |
| +--------------+ |                | +-----------------+                      +-----------------+ |
|     Electron     |                |                         GraalVM                              |
+------------------+                +--------------------------------------------------------------+
```

Workflow:
- IDE client creates a `YjsChannel` backed by Yjs `Y.Array` allowing bidirectional message passing
- IDE client connects to the Ydoc server using the [Yjs WebSocket provider](https://docs.yjs.dev/ecosystem/connection-provider/y-websocket)
- Ydoc server creates a corresponding `YjsChannel` that is synchronized with the client's channel and passes it to the Language server via `YjsChannelCallbacks` callback

This way IDE client and the Language Server establish `YjsChannel` connection and start exchanging messages.

### Important Notes

linked_issues.title: Avoid networking layer of Akka by `ydoc-server` talking directly to the engine+ls
linked_issues.body:
### Related Prior Work
 
- now when #13178 has been integrated
- #5419 was dreaming about:
  - using Helidon to implement network communication
  - writing Language Server in JavaScript
- since #10783 & co. Helidon can be used for `WebSocket` emulation
- discussion about [using Y.js to simulate duplex network communication](https://discord.com/channels/401396655599124480/1425766988839653377/1425770149683789946)

### Task 1: Avoid two WebSocket Connections

- modify IDE & `ydoc-shared` code
- avoid IDE creating two `WebSocket` connections (one to `ydoc-server` another to language server_)
- let IDE connect to `ydoc-server` only and ...
- ... "send messages" by adding them to FIFO Y.js queue
- let the `ydoc-server` distribute these messages to language server
- sketch of necessary changes: [Connect MainModule.scala and languageServer.ts so they can all to each other](https://github.com/enso-org/enso/compare/develop...JaroslavTulach:enso:YdocDirect)

### Task 2: Ydoc Server Talks to Language Server Directly

- with #13178 the Ydoc server no longer has to send network messages
- but it can make direct calls into _language server_
- by doing so we can eliminate the _network Akka_ layer

### Future Work: Less Messages

- as @farmaazon puts it:
- _we could do even better. Many endpoints are for altering some state managed by LS (execution context, file contents...). We should just update structures representing that state._
- all that's going to be enabled once we finish tasks 1 and 2

title: [feat] add ntfy as a new alert notification channel
body:
### What is changed and how it works?

This PR adds [ntfy](https://ntfy.sh) as a new alert notification channel
(type=15) to HertzBeat. ntfy is a popular open-source HTTP-based
push-notification service that is widely used in self-hosting communities,
and it works well for receiving HertzBeat alerts on mobile devices and
desktops without depending on third-party platforms.

#### Backend changes
- New handler `NtfyAlertNotifyHandlerImpl` in `hertzbeat-alerter`,
  extending `AbstractAlertNotifyHandlerImpl` with type id `15`
- Three new fields on `NoticeReceiver`: `ntfyServerUrl`, `ntfyTopic`,
  `ntfyToken`
- New configurable property `alerter.ntfy-default-server-url`
  (default: `https://ntfy.sh`)

#### Feature highlights
- **Priority mapping** — alert severity is mapped to ntfy priority levels
  (`critical`=5, `warning`=4, `info`/unknown=3, resolved alerts=2)
- **Emoji tags** — status- and severity-aware tags
  (`rotating_light` + `skull` for critical firing, `warning` for warning,
  `white_check_mark` for resolved); the alertname is also appended as a
  plain-text tag
- **Bearer token auth** — supports self-hosted ntfy instances with
  access-control tokens
- **Click action** — notifications carry a click URL pointing to the
  HertzBeat console
- **Markdown body** — alert content is rendered in Markdown for
  better readability on ntfy clients

#### Frontend changes
- Added ntfy option to the notification receiver type dropdown
- Three new form fields: server URL (optional, default
  `https://ntfy.sh`), topic (required), access token (optional)
- Display tag in the receiver list table
- i18n translations for en-US, zh-CN, zh-TW, ja-JP, pt-BR

#### Tests
Added `NtfyAlertNotifyHandlerImplTest` with 16 test cases covering:
- Successful send / non-2xx response / network errors
- URL building (custom server, default server fallback,
  trailing-slash handling)
- Bearer token header injection
- Priority mapping for all severity levels and resolved state
- Tag generation for critical/warning/info/resolved cases
- Header presence and values (Priority, Tags, Click, Markdown)

### Checklist

- [x] I have read the [Contribution Guidelines](https://github.com/apache/hertzbeat/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md)
- [x] I have self-reviewed my code
- [x] I have added unit tests covering the new feature
- [x] My change follows the existing alert handler conventions
      (Gotify/ServerChan style)
- [x] No HIP is required (new notification channel — small enhancement)

title: [feat] add API token management functionality
body:
## What's changed?

Previously, there was no way to manage HertzBeat API tokens once they were generated, which led to the following issues:

1. Once a `Token` is shared with a third party for alert integration or log ingestion, **the access permissions for that Token cannot be revoked**, which poses a security risk.
2. The `Token` generation button is scattered across various integration pages (alert integration, log integration), giving the impression that it is a feature specific to each integration rather than a global setting, which can easily confuse users.
3. There is a lack of visibility regarding `Tokens`; it is unclear how many `Tokens` are in use within the system, who created them, or whether they are still actively in use.

## Modification Details

1. **Persistent Storage of API Tokens**: Do not store plaintext; display masked versions; record the creator, creation time, expiration time, and last access time.
2. **API Token Revocation Validation Filter**: Runs after `Sureness` authentication to check whether the authenticated `Token` has been revoked. (Existing `Tokens` are not affected; backward compatibility is maintained.)
3. **API Token Expiration Time**: When generating the token, you can select an expiration time (7, 30, 90, 180, or 365 days, or never expire). The expiration time is written to the JWT `exp` claim and is natively validated by the `Sureness` framework.
4. **Permissions Configuration Update (sureness.yml)**: The /api/account/token endpoints remain admin-only, and the controller layer keeps the same restriction. Only administrators can generate, list, and revoke API tokens; administrators can revoke any token.

5. **Managed Token Identification**: Newly generated API tokens carry a dedicated managed=true JWT claim. Only these managed tokens are subject to database-backed lifecycle checks; legacy tokens without this claim continue to work for backward compatibility.

6. **Runtime Account and Role Revalidation**: For managed tokens, each authenticated request revalidates the token owner’s current account status and role set. If the account is disabled, removed, or no longer has the required roles, the token is rejected even if the JWT itself has not yet expired.

7. **Fail-Closed Revocation Checking**: Revocation and account-state validation no longer fail open. If token status validation cannot be completed because of a runtime/storage error, the request is denied instead of being silently allowed. lastUsedTime updates remain best-effort and do not block requests.

8. **Managed Token Lifecycle Tracking**: Managed tokens now support full lifecycle operations, including generation, listing, revocation, last-access updates, and expiration-based invalidation, while keeping legacy token behavior unchanged.

9. **Frontend and E2E Alignment**: The frontend token management flow and E2E tests were updated to match the new semantics, including explicit “never expire” handling and end-to-end coverage for token generation, usage, listing, and revocation.


## Checklist

- [x]  I have read the [Contributing Guide](https://hertzbeat.apache.org/docs/community/code_style_and_quality_guide)
- [ ]  I have written the necessary doc or comment.
- [x]  I have added the necessary unit tests and all cases have passed.

## Add or update API

- [x] I have added the necessary [e2e tests](https://github.com/apache/hertzbeat/tree/master/e2e) and all cases have passed.

> API Token Generation
![save](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2a768771-39fb-413c-a673-51b9e9b972c0)

> API token expiration validation
![check](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/584f0243-53d9-4322-853f-df44980c8389)

title: [bugfix&improve] Fix alert label persistence and improve custom label input
body:
## What's changed?

### Related Issue
Fixes #3901

### Overview
This PR fixes multiple issues in the alert definition label system, including UI display problems and incorrect persistence of system built-in labels. It improves both backend data correctness and frontend usability by clearly separating system labels from user-defined custom labels and enhancing the label selector experience.

---

### Backend Changes

#### Fix: Prevent System Built-in Labels from Being Persisted
**Problem**  
When creating or updating alert definitions, system built-in labels (such as alert mode and severity labels) were incorrectly saved to the `Label` table. These labels are auto-generated by the system and should not be persisted as user-defined labels, leading to data pollution and incorrect label management.

**Solution**
- Introduced `isSystemBuiltInLabel()` method in `AlertDefineServiceImpl` to identify system built-in labels
- Filtered out all system built-in labels before saving labels to the database
- Only persist user-defined custom labels when creating or updating alert definitions
- System built-in labels include:
  - `CommonConstants.ALERT_MODE_LABEL`
  - `CommonConstants.LABEL_ALERT_SEVERITY`

**Affected Files**
- `hertzbeat-alerter/src/main/java/org/apache/hertzbeat/alert/service/impl/AlertDefineServiceImpl.java`

#### Refactor: Enable Cross-Module Label Reuse
- Moved `LabelDao` and `LabelService` from `hertzbeat-manager` to `hertzbeat-base` module
- This refactor enables shared label persistence logic across modules and avoids duplicated implementations

#### Feature: Auto-persist New Custom Labels
- Added `saveNewCustomLabel()` method in `AlertDefineServiceImpl`
- Automatically persists newly created custom labels when alert definitions are created or updated

---

### Frontend Changes

#### Fix & Improvement: Alert Label Selector Component
**Problems**
- Auto-added labels from severity selection were not properly displayed in the UI
- Custom labels entered in one row could appear in dropdown options of other rows (cross-row pollution)
- Users were unable to reliably add free-text custom labels

**Solutions**
- Enabled free-text custom label input by setting `labelIsCustom: true` in the alert-setting component
- Introduced `activeSearchIndex` and `activeSearchType` to precisely track the currently active input field
- Optimized custom option display logic to ensure custom labels and values only appear in their corresponding rows
- Enhanced `writeValue()` to correctly extract and rebuild `labelKeys` and `labelMap` from existing alert definitions
  - Ensures system built-in labels (e.g. `alertMode`, `alert.severity`) are correctly displayed for selection but not persisted
- Improved `onKeyChange()` and `onValueChange()` to automatically add newly entered custom label keys and values to the option list for reuse
- Enhanced `onSearch()` to clear stale custom input values when the search field is cleared, preventing leftover dropdown pollution

**Affected Files**
- `web-app/src/app/shared/components/label-selector/label-selector.component.html`
- `web-app/src/app/shared/components/label-selector/label-selector.component.ts`

---

### Result
- System built-in labels are no longer persisted, ensuring clean and correct label data
- Auto-generated labels are properly displayed and selectable in the UI
- Users can add and reuse custom labels through free-text input
- Label selector behavior is isolated per row, eliminating cross-row side effects


## Screenshot
![20260106-010118](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a5ea1f55-169e-444b-9578-d8ec72462912)

## Checklist

- [x]  I have read the [Contributing Guide](https://hertzbeat.apache.org/docs/community/code_style_and_quality_guide)
- [ ]  I have written the necessary doc or comment.
- [ ]  I have added the necessary unit tests and all cases have passed.

title: [ZEPPELIN-6429] Focus paragraph editor on clone/insert in New UI
body:
### What is this PR for?
After cloning or inserting a paragraph in the New UI, the cursor stays on the wrapper element instead of the editor, so you have to click before typing. This focuses the new paragraph's editor one tick after `PARAGRAPH_ADDED`, gated to clone/insert initiated by this client so auto-append on run and other clients' inserts don't steal focus. It also skips dirty-marking on programmatic editor `setValue` (`isFlush`) so the cloned content isn't discarded. (The clone *content* loss itself is handled separately in https://github.com/apache/zeppelin/pull/5254#pullrequestreview-4415596876; this covers the *cursor* part.)

title: [ZEPPELIN-6411]  Semantic search for Zeppelin
body: ### What is this PR for? Added `EmbeddingSearch` — a new `SearchService` implementation that enables natural language search across Zeppelin notebooks using ONNX-based sentence embeddings (all-MiniLM-L6-v2). Disabled by default, enabled with `zeppelin.search.semantic.enable = true`. **The problem**: Zeppelin's built-in search uses Lucene's keyword matching, which works well for exact terms but falls short for the way analysts actually search. A user looking for "yesterday's spending" gets zero results — even though their notebooks contain SELECT sum(cost) WHERE date = current_date - interval '1' day. The words don't match, so Lucene can't find it. This PR adds EmbeddingSearch, an alternative SearchService that uses sentence embeddings (all-MiniLM-L6-v2 via ONNX Runtime) to match by meaning instead of keywords. It runs entirely in-process with no external services required. Beyond semantic matching, EmbeddingSearch addresses other gaps in notebook search: - Indexes paragraph output — table results and text output become searchable, not just the code - Extracts SQL table names — FROM/JOIN references are extracted and used to boost related paragraphs in a two-phase ranking - Strips interpreter prefixes — %spark.sql, %python etc. are removed so they don't pollute search results - Live indexing — new or updated paragraphs are searchable immediately, no restart needed ### What type of PR is it? Feature ### Todos - [x] EmbeddingSearch core implementation (ONNX inference, mean pooling, cosine similarity) - [x] Table name extraction from SQL (FROM/JOIN regex) with two-phase search boosting - [x] Paragraph output indexing (TABLE, TEXT results) - [x] Versioned binary persistence (v3 format) - [x] Live indexing (new paragraphs searchable immediately) - [x] Angular UI: render search results with separate code/output/tables blocks - [x] Classic UI: same improvements - [x] 11 unit tests including semantic validation - [x] Documentation ### What is the Jira issue? - https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/ZEPPELIN-6411 ### How should this be tested? **Automated tests:** ```bash # Embedding search tests (requires ~86MB model download, one-time) ZEPPELIN_EMBEDDING_TEST=true mvn test -pl zeppelin-zengine -Dtest=EmbeddingSearchTest # Verify no regressions to existing Lucene search mvn test -pl zeppelin-zengine -Dtest=LuceneSearchTest Manual testing: 1. Set zeppelin.search.semantic.enable = true in zeppelin-site.xml 2. Restart Zeppelin 3. Search for natural language queries like: - "yesterday's spending" (Lucene: 0 results → Semantic: finds spend queries) - "how much do drivers earn" (finds taxi tip analysis) - "late deliveries" (finds shipping performance queries) - "airport rides" (both work — keyword match exists) ``` ### Screenshots (if appropriate) Semantic Search with New UI Semantic Search with Classic UI ### Questions: - Does the license files need to update? - Yes — NOTICE updated with ONNX Runtime (MIT) and DJL Tokenizers (Apache 2.0) attribution. - Is there breaking changes for older versions? - No. Disabled by default. Existing LuceneSearch behavior is unchanged.
