linked_issues.title: Python automation API: "unknown provider package" error
linked_issues.body:
### What happened?

Using the Python automation API with inline programs and parameterized providers (e.g. bridged Terraform providers), sequential operations such as `preview` followed by `up` should both succeed. Instead, the second operation fails with `unknown provider package '<uuid>'`.

The error seems to happen because the Python SDK caches the package reference UUID returned by `RegisterPackage` during the first operation. When the second operation starts, it creates a new engine process, but the SDK reuses the stale cached UUID from the previous engine session.

### Example

Reproduction repo: https://gist.github.com/imiric/1eec339ac459656ffc9b4f5d0d54524c

See `repro.log`.

### Output of `pulumi about`

```
CLI
Version      3.226.0
Go Version   go1.26.1
Go Compiler  gc

Plugins
KIND      NAME                VERSION
language  python              3.226.0
resource  terraform-provider  1.1.0

Host
OS       void
Version
Arch     x86_64

This project is written in python: executable='/home/user/pulumi-python-repro/.venv/bin/python3' version='3.14.3' toolchain='Pip' toolchainVersion='26.0.1' typechecker='None'

Backend
Name           work
URL            file://~
User           user
Organizations
Token type     personal

Dependencies:
NAME         VERSION
pip          26.0.1
pulumi-null  3.2.3
```

### Additional context

I'm able to work around the issue by resetting the `packageRef` before each operation:
```python
import pulumi_null._utilities as _utils
_utils._package_ref = ...
```

### Related issues

- Go: https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/issues/22168
- Java: https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi-java/issues/2003

### Contributing

Vote on this issue by adding a 👍 reaction. 
To contribute a fix for this issue, leave a comment (and link to your pull request, if you've opened one already).

title: Fix RenderDocs to elide newlines if origionally missing
body:
This fixes `RenderDocs` to elide a trailing new line if the original text had no trailing new line.

This flagged up that a lot of python comments were both relying on this, and had extra new lines that were unneeded.

This makes it easier to work with functions built on top of RenderDocs to do partial string fixes (like handling refs, examples etc) without having to worry about newlines being added all the time.
author: Frassle

title: [programgen] Generate map ranges as key-indexed collections (Python & NodeJS)
body:
## Description

Stacked on #23634.

A resource with a map `range` option was collected into an ordered list (Python `list`, NodeJS array) and then referenced by its map key (`mapResource["k1"]`), which does not type-check — a list/array can't be indexed by a string. Python failed mypy `__getitem__` overload; NodeJS failed `Property 'k1' does not exist on type 'Target[]'`.

Generate a **key-indexed, strongly-typed** collection for map ranges:

- **Python:** `dict[str, T]` populated by `m[range['key']] = ...`
- **NodeJS:** `{[key: string]: T}` populated by `m[range.key] = ...`

Detection reuses the existing inline map check at the range site (`pcl.UnwrapOption(rangeExpr.Type()).(*model.MapType)`) — no new helper.

### Also in this PR

- **Tighten list typing:** numeric/list ranges are now `list[T]` in Python instead of `list[Any]` (NodeJS already used `T[]`).
- **Unique Python loop variable per range:** each range statement binds `<resource>_range` instead of the shared `range` name. Reusing `range` type-locked its element type across loops — a numeric range fixed it to `dict[str, int]`, which then rejected a later map range's string `range['key']` as a dict key. Unique names remove the coupling (and stop shadowing the `range` builtin). NodeJS already block-scopes each loop's `range` with `const`, so it needs no change.

`l3-range-map-ref` now type-checks under mypy and pyright and compiles under tsc, and is removed from the Python and NodeJS expected-failure lists. Go has unrelated range program-generation bugs and is left failing.

## Checklist

- [x] Conformance: full `l3-range*` family passes for Python (Default/TOML/Classes) and NodeJS (TSC/TSNode/Bun)
- [x] `pkg/codegen` program-gen goldens regenerated (components-pp, python-regress-14037-pp, unknown-resource-pp) and pass
- [x] Changelog entry added
author: iwahbe

title: [programgen] Guard references to conditionally-created resources (Python & NodeJS)
body:
## Description

Stacked on #23632 (the `l3-range-ref` test split).

A resource declared with a boolean `range` option is created zero or one times, so its program variable is optional. Generated programs that dereferenced such a resource were not well-typed:

- **Python** emitted `bool_resource.name` on a `Target | None` value → mypy `union-attr` error.
- **NodeJS** emitted `boolResource?.name`, widening the result to `Output<string> | undefined`, which is not assignable to a required `Input<string>`.

This detects the dereference in a shared PCL helper, `pcl.IsOptionalResource`, and emits language-appropriate code:

- **Python** (`pkg/codegen/pcl/rewrite_optional_resource.go` + `pkg/codegen/python`): wraps the access — and any apply over it — as `expr if root is not None else None`, run *after* applies are rewritten so the guard surrounds the apply rather than being hoisted inside it. Equality against `null` is now rendered with `is`/`is not`, which is idiomatic and lets the type checker narrow.
- **NodeJS** (`pkg/codegen/nodejs`): emits a non-null assertion (`boolResource!.name`) at the optional-resource root, yielding `Output<string>`. Genuinely-optional schema properties keep optional chaining.

The two languages need different shapes because their generated inputs differ: Python resource inputs are `Optional`-typed (so the `None` fallback is assignable), whereas NodeJS inputs are required (so only a non-null assertion type-checks).

`l3-range-bool-ref` now type-checks under mypy and pyright and compiles under tsc, and is removed from the Python and NodeJS conformance expected-failure lists. Go has unrelated range program-generation bugs and is left failing. `l3-range-map-ref` (a separate collection-shape issue) is out of scope.

The `is`/`is not` rendering also updates a few existing program goldens (`l2-resource-optional`, `l2-primitive-ref-optional`, `l1-config-types-object`) from `== None` to `is None`.

## Checklist

- [x] Conformance: `l3-range-bool-ref` passes for Python (Default/TOML/Classes) and NodeJS (TSC/TSNode/Bun)
- [x] Affected program goldens regenerated and pass
- [x] `pkg/codegen` program-gen golden batches unaffected
- [x] Changelog entry added
author: iwahbe

title: [test] Split l3-range-ref conformance test by range kind
body:
## Description

Splits the `l3-range-ref` language conformance test into three focused tests so that each range-reference codegen path can be marked pass/fail independently per language. Previously all four reference patterns lived in one program, so a single broken pattern forced the whole test into a language's `expectedFailures`, masking the others.

New tests:

- **`l3-range-list-ref`** — numeric and list ranges, indexed references (`numResource[0]`, `listResource[1]`), and dynamic indexing (`listResource[range.key]`, the #14037 quote/index-rewrite path).
- **`l3-range-map-ref`** — a map range indexed by a string key (`mapResource["k1"]`).
- **`l3-range-bool-ref`** — dereferencing a conditionally-created (boolean `range`) resource (`boolResource.name`).

This PR is a pure test refactor — **no program-generation changes**. Per-language status is recorded exactly as it stands today:

| Test | PCL | Python | NodeJS | Go |
|------|-----|--------|--------|----|
| `l3-range-list-ref` | ✅ pass | ✅ pass | ✅ pass | ❌ expected-fail (syntax errors) |
| `l3-range-map-ref` | ✅ pass | ❌ expected-fail (`__getitem__` overload) | ❌ expected-fail (`Property 'k1'`) | ❌ expected-fail |
| `l3-range-bool-ref` | ✅ pass | ❌ expected-fail (`union-attr`) | ❌ expected-fail (`Output<string> \| undefined`) | ❌ expected-fail |

Follow-up #23634 (stacked on this PR) fixes the Python and NodeJS program generation so `l3-range-bool-ref` passes for both, removing it from their expected-failure lists.

## Checklist

- [x] Conformance suites run locally for PCL, Python (Default/TOML/Classes), NodeJS (TSC/TSNode/Bun), and Go
- [x] `expectedFailures` updated per language; `l3-range-list-ref` goldens committed
author: iwahbe

title: Implement MultiArgumentInputs for functions in Python SDK-gen and program-gen
body:
Fixes #23549.

Python SDK-gen already emits a positional signature for invokes, spreading each input into its own keyword parameter and packing them into the named property bag on the wire. `multiArgumentInputs` therefore needs no distinct codegen path in Python — the only thing standing in the way was a hard error guarding functions (and program-gen methods and invokes) that set the flag.

Removing those guards lets the existing generators produce the expected positional invoke for a `multiArgumentInputs` function:

```python
def multi_argument_invoke(first, second=None, opts=None) -> ...
```

Python is exercised end-to-end by the `l2-invoke-multi-argument` conformance test (added in #23570); it is removed from that test's expected failures and its snapshots are generated.
author: iwahbe

linked_issues.title: [python] Implement MultiArgumentInputs for functions in SDK-gen and program-gen
linked_issues.body:
## What happens?

Python SDK generation fails with a hard error when a schema function sets `multiArgumentInputs`:

```
python SDK-gen does not implement MultiArgumentInputs for function '%s'
```

(`pkg/codegen/python/gen.go`.) Program-gen errors the same way for both functions and methods (`pkg/codegen/python/gen_program_expressions.go`).

Node.js implements both SDK-gen and program-gen for multi-argument functions, and .NET implements SDK-gen, so the schema feature is established — Python is a gap.

## Why it matters

pulumi/pulumi-terraform-bridge#1955 bridges Terraform provider-defined functions (Terraform 1.8+, positional parameters by definition) into Pulumi invokes using `multiArgumentInputs`. Bridged providers always ship Python SDKs, so this blocks that work.

## Expected behavior

For a function with `multiArgumentInputs`, Python SDK-gen should emit a positional signature (e.g. `parse_arn(arn, ...)`) packing the arguments into the named property bag on the wire, and program-gen should emit the corresponding positional call.

title: Replace this-keyword-resource-attr program-gen test with a conformance test
body:
## Description

The `this-keyword-resource-attr` program-gen test bound against the real, multi-megabyte `aws-5.4.0.json` schema. Its only unique coverage was a NodeJS codegen concern: when a PCL resource is named `this` inside a component, the generated `ComponentResource` class must rename the resource variable (to `_this`) while keeping the `parent: this` receiver pointer intact.

- A top-level resource named `this` is already covered by `l2-resource-keyword-overlap`.
- Component generation with `parent: this` is already covered by the `l3-component-*` tests.
- No existing test combined the two — which is the one thing this program-gen test added.

This PR re-expresses that coverage as a synthetic-provider conformance test, `l3-resource-keyword-overlap`: a component whose child resource is named `this`, referenced by a second resource. Unlike the program-gen test (which only diffs generated source against the real AWS SDK), the conformance suite generates **and runs** the program across every language, so the rename is verified end to end.

The generated NodeJS component is exactly the target scenario:

```ts
const _this = new simple.Resource(`${name}-this`, {value: args.input}, { parent: this });
const dependent = new simple.Resource(`${name}-dependent`, {value: _this.value}, { parent: this });
```

## Per-language results

| Language | Result |
| --- | --- |
| PCL | pass |
| NodeJS (tsc / tsnode / bun) | pass |
| Python (mypy / default) | pass |
| Go | known failure — same component input/output typing codegen bugs as `l3-component-nested` (bool literal not a `pulumi.BoolInput`; `BoolOutput` assigned to `AnyOutput`) |
| Python pyright (toml / classes) | skipped — same component-args optional-property (`reportTypedDictNotRequiredAccess`) issue already skipped for `l3-component-simple` |

No goldens are committed for the skipped languages.

This is part of the effort to remove real-provider schemas from the codegen test corpus by migrating their unique coverage to synthetic conformance tests. (The `aws-5.4.0.json` schema still has many other consumers and is not removed here.)

## Checklist

- [x] No changelog entry — test-only change with no user-visible impact (`impact/no-changelog-required`).
author: iwahbe

title: Fold the optional-complex-config program test into l1-config-types-object
body:
> [!NOTE]
> This change is AI generated.

Part of the follow-up to #23507 (step 2 of https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/pull/23507#pullrequestreview-4476628697): replace the synthetic aws-5.4.0 program tests with real conformance coverage.

The `optional-complex-config` program test only checked the generated TypeScript for optional (null-defaulted) config values with complex types (`config.getObject<T>`), without compiling or running it. The `l1-config-types-object` conformance test already covers typed and untyped object config values, so this PR:

- extends `l1-config-types-object` with three null-defaulted complex config values: `optionalList list(string)`, `optionalMap map(string)`, and a heterogeneous `optionalObject object({prop=string, other=int})`;
- runs the test twice over the same source (`RunsShareSource`): once with the optional values unset so their null defaults apply, and once with them set;
- deletes the `optional-complex-config` program test.

The outputs are JSON-encoded with an explicit null check so unset values surface as the string `"null"` rather than as top-level null stack outputs (#19015). The null check has to be explicit because TypeScript's `JSON.stringify(undefined)` is `undefined`, which silently drops the export — exactly the kind of cross-language inconsistency #19015 tracks. The assertions use `assert.JSONEq` for the set values since JSON whitespace and key order differ between language runtimes.

The old test's `vpcTag`/`vpcId` configs (optional *string* getters) aren't carried over: they're already covered by `l1-config-types-primitive`. The old test ran for NodeJS and Dotnet; pulumi-dotnet picks up the folded coverage via its conformance run on the next pulumi/pulumi bump. Go continues to skip `l1-config-types-object` (pre-existing "fails to compile" entry), so no Go snapshots change.

Snapshots were regenerated with `scripts/run-conformance.sh l1-config-types-object` and verified by a second run without `PULUMI_ACCEPT`; `make lint_golang` is clean.
author: iwahbe

title: Fix TestRunNeoIntegration_DoubleCtrlCExits flake on slow runners
body:
Fixes #23541.

## Root cause

Not a regression of the cancel-on-TUI-exit fix — a test-harness timing flake. The failure in the linked run happened on a starved `windows-latest` acceptance job (attempt 1; the rerun passed). The test's quit-driver goroutine polled for the SSE connect for only 2s and called `p.Quit()` **only if** the `tea.Program` had already been constructed — on a slow runner where `runNeo` hadn't gotten that far yet, nothing ever quit the TUI, making the 5s `t.Fatal` a certainty.

## Changes

- The quit goroutine now waits for the program, then the SSE connect, under a generous shared deadline, and always quits once the program exists.
- All hardcoded 2s/5s waits in the file use one 30s constant: these tests guard against permanent hangs, so longer deadlines lose no signal but stop flaking under load.
- `scripts/go-test.py`: open the gotestsum JSON as UTF-8 (Windows cp1252 chokes on non-ASCII test output) and rename the inner `except ... as e`, which unbound the outer `e` and turned `raise e` into the `NameError` seen in the issue's log.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
author: VenelinMartinov

title: Remove the awsx-1.0.0-beta.5 schema
body:
Part of the effort to remove multi-megabyte real-provider schemas from `tests/testdata/codegen` so the corpus is small enough to `//go:embed`. Follows #23492, #23493, #23496, #23498, and #23502; only `aws-5.4.0.json` remains after this.

Removes `awsx-1.0.0-beta.5.json` (182K) and migrates its consumers so no coverage is lost:

- **regress-11176** (Go import-conflict regression, #11176): deleted. The `l2-name-conflicts` conformance test already covers the same scenario — two packages that both define a module named `mod`, forcing generated Go programs to disambiguate the conflicting imports — and is stronger, since it also compiles and runs the generated program. The import-renaming logic additionally remains unit-tested in `gen_program_test.go`.
- **csharp-plain-lists**: deleted. The `l2-plain` conformance test already exercises a plain list of plain objects via its `dataList` input, but only ever passed an empty list; its program now passes a non-empty list, giving compile-and-run coverage in every language instead of a C#-only golden. Generating this exposes a pre-existing Go program-gen bug (map literals nested inside plain list elements render without a type and do not compile), so `l2-plain` is registered as an expected failure for Go — with its Go goldens removed — until that is fixed in a follow-up.
- **TestResolvingPackageReferences** and **TestTransitivePackageReferencesAreLoadedFromTopLevelResourceDefinition**: now use `remoteref` → `goalias`. The `remoteref` schema gains a property referencing a goalias *type*, since transitive package-reference detection only registers foreign object types, not foreign resources; this also extends `TestRoundtripRemoteTypeRef` (#13000) to cover a remote type reference in addition to a remote resource reference.
- **transpiled awsx-fargate**: deleted; it was a Python-only smoke test whose coverage is provided by the remaining program tests.
- Cleanup: awsx entry removed from the test host and the Makefile `get_schemas` list; dead awsx exclusion removed from the schema docs test.

Verified locally: full `pkg/codegen/...` suite with `-tags all` passes; l2-plain conformance passes for pcl, nodejs, and python and is skipped for Go as an expected failure; `make lint_golang` is clean.

title: Fix memory leaks & crashes when loading multiple Go extensions in one Python process
body:
Relates-to: https://github.com/go-python/gopy/issues/392 
Fixes: https://github.com/go-python/gopy/issues/385
Fixes: https://github.com/go-python/gopy/issues/370

---

I took [Scusemua](https://github.com/Scusemua)'s two commits in https://github.com/go-python/gopy/pull/361 as a base and built a broader set of fixes on top, covering memory leaks, GC coordination, and shared library conflicts.

This PR wouldn't exist without the work Scusemua put in first. His two commits from July 2024 fixed a subtle but nasty bug: Go code was calling a Python function without holding the GIL.

That's a precise fix that prevents several hard-to-reproduce crashes, and everything else here builds on top of it.

---

I added four fixes in the same area:

1. C string memory leaks. Generated code allocated C strings when reading string fields from Go structs, slices, and maps, but never freed them. Switched those paths to a helper that frees after Python copies the value.

2. Unsafe GC call. The generated wrapper was calling Go's GC from a thread that crosses the C/Go boundary, which could panic on multi-core machines. Rerouted it through a background goroutine via a channel.

3. GC out of sync. Without coordination, Go heap objects piled up between Python GC cycles. Fixed by registering a gc.callbacks handler that triggers a Go collection after each Python one.

4. Symbol conflicts between co-loaded extensions. Loading two gopy-built shared libraries with RTLD_GLOBAL caused one library's Go internals to clobber the other's, corrupting heap state. Fixed by loading without that flag so each library's runtime stays isolated.

5. I added a regression test for two extensions in a single Python process, stealing from the discussion in issue 370. 

I also expanded the CI matrix to cover more Go versions and macOS configs.

title: Modernize CI: new Go versions, C23 fix, Windows testing
body:
This PR consolidates 6 stacked changes that modernize the CI pipeline and fix compatibility issues with newer compilers and Go versions. ## Summary of changes - **Modernize GitHub Actions** — upgrade all actions to current versions, pin Python to 3.11, remove AppVeyor, skip two known-broken CGO tests - **Enable CI on stacked/feature branches** — `pull_request` trigger now runs on PRs targeting any branch, not just `master` - **Expand Go version matrix** — add Go 1.24.x and 1.25.x to CI - **Fix C23 compiler compatibility** — guard `typedef uint8_t bool;` against C23's native `bool` to fix build errors with newer C compilers - **Update `golang.org/x/tools`** — bump from v0.16.0 → v0.29.0, required for Go 1.25.x compatibility - **Resume Windows CI** — re-enable Windows runner, fix LF line-ending issue that broke `TestCheckSupportMatrix`, add Windows test docs ---
[1/6] Update GitHub Actions and skip failing CGO tests — go-python/gopy#382 **Changes:** - Remove `appveyor.yml` - Upgrade `actions/checkout` v2 → v4 - Upgrade `actions/setup-go` v2 → v5 (with built-in caching, removes separate cache step) - Add `actions/setup-python@v5` pinned to Python 3.11 - Upgrade `codecov/codecov-action` v1 → v4 - Skip `TestBindSimple` and `TestBindCgoPackage` **Why Python 3.11?** Pinning avoids Python 3.12 breakage while we focus on infrastructure. The two skipped tests fail due to a known Go 1.21+ CGO limitation when multiple C-shared libraries are loaded in the same process (see go-python/gopy#370). Similar to go-python/gopy#378 by @coffeemakingtoaster. Co-authored-by: @coffeemakingtoaster
[2/6] Enable CI for stacked PRs targeting any branch — b-long/gopy#4 Updates the `pull_request` trigger from `branches: [master]` to `branches: ['**']` so GitHub Actions CI runs on stacked PRs that target feature branches rather than only `master`.
[3/6] Add Go 1.24.x to CI test matrix — b-long/gopy#5 Adds Go 1.24.x alongside existing 1.22.x and 1.21.x matrix entries. Based on go-python/gopy#378 by @coffeemakingtoaster. Note: two tests remain skipped due to known Go 1.21+ CGO issues (expected). Co-authored-by: Max
[4/6] Add C23 compatibility for bool typedef — b-long/gopy#6 Wraps `typedef uint8_t bool;` with `#ifndef __cplusplus` / `#if !defined(bool)` preprocessor guards to avoid conflicts with C23's native `bool` type. This fixes compilation errors with newer C compilers that default to the C23 standard. Based on go-python/gopy#379 by @deuill. Co-authored-by: Marid de Uill
[5/6] Add Go 1.25.x to CI test matrix and update dependencies — b-long/gopy#8 Adds Go 1.25.x alongside 1.24.x, 1.22.x, and 1.21.x. Upgrades `golang.org/x/tools` from v0.16.0 → v0.29.0 and updates the `go.mod` `go` directive to `1.22.0` (set by `go mod tidy`). **Why the dependency update?** Go 1.25 introduced breaking changes that make `golang.org/x/tools v0.16.0` incompatible: ``` golang.org/x/tools@v0.16.0/internal/tokeninternal/tokeninternal.go:78:9: invalid array length -delta * delta (constant -256 of type int64) ``` Upgrading to v0.29.0 resolves this.
[6/6] Resume Windows testing — b-long/gopy#10 Re-enables the Windows runner and addresses two issues that were blocking it: 1. **LF line endings** — adds `.gitattributes` enforcing `eol=lf` for text files. `TestCheckSupportMatrix` does a byte-level comparison of `SUPPORT_MATRIX.md`; on Windows, Git's CRLF conversion caused spurious failures even though the content was correct. 2. **Test matrix** — simplifies and expands the matrix configuration. 3. **Docs** — adds notes to `CONTRIBUTING.md` / test docs on installing `psutil` before running the test suite on Windows.
--- ## Attribution Several changes in this PR are based on or inspired by prior community contributions: | Upstream PR | Author | Topic | |---|---|---| | go-python/gopy#378 | @coffeemakingtoaster | Actions modernization & Go 1.24 | | go-python/gopy#379 | @deuill | C23 bool typedef fix |

title: bind: Check for exceptions and propagate errors back to python caller
body:
This is  WIP attempt to fix the issue of errors not being propagated back to the python caller as a proper exception. The problem occurs because the wrapped function will check for an error from the Go calls and use `PyErr_SetString` but still return a value. Then the code generated by PyBindGen will only get the retval and return it. This results in:

```
SystemError: <built-in function mymodule.MyFunction> returned a result with an error set
```

The fixes in this PR add a custom `Function` wrapper to the PyBindGen code that adds the function wrappers. In this `Function` class we update the `generate_call()` to do an error check after the wrapped function call. The result of this fix is that the tests that had previously been set up to expect the generic `SystemError` have now been updated to expect the real Go error as an exception.

Known issues:

1. Each function wrapper now checks `PyErr_Occurred()` before returning. I've added the ability for a custom `retval` check first if performance is a concern, but I don't yet know where that expression can be added. As an example, the `CgoHandle` long value uses a `-1` when its not valid. So the failure expression for the function *could* be `retval == -1` which would check that first before calling `PyErr_Occurred()`. This may not be worth the effort.
2. ~~We now could have a potential memory leak during an exception. As an example, a wrapped function that returns a string will generate code that returns `C.CString("")` on error. Before this PR the object is always returned as a python object which then takes ownership of the `char*`. If we now do an error check and return `NULL` then there is not yet any cleanup code that will free that `char*`. I am not yet sure how to add the cleanup code. Ideally on error we would not allocate an empty `char*` and just return `NULL`. It may be easier to fix that part of the code gen then to try and generate C wrapper cleanup code for the `retval`.~~

Refs #254

linked_issues.title: Slices of structs are exposed as handles instead of wrapped instances
linked_issues.body:
In response to [my question here](https://github.com/go-python/gopy/pull/180#issuecomment-521049840), I have filed an issue with an example case.

Refs #180 

Test project:
[gopytest.zip](https://github.com/go-python/gopy/files/3503660/gopytest.zip)

Building using python-2.7, with 

```
gopy pkg -name='gopytest' -output=./dist gopytest/pyapi
```

The project contains an "api" package, and then a "pyapi" package meant to export a `Request` capability over the api, to python.

```python
from gopytest import pyapi
req = pyapi.Request()
req.Count=2
ret = req.FetchPeople()
print(ret)
# pyapi.Slice_Ptr_api_Person([6, 7])
```

I find that instead of getting back a list of `Person` instance, I would get a list of handles.
`Person` class is actually not discovered from the import of "api" and exported, so I had to do a type alias in "pyapi" to end up seeing a python `Person` class. Then I was able to manually map the handles to instances by constructing `Person(handle=i)`.

Now if I try building both packages:

```
gopy pkg -name='gopytest' -output=./dist gopytest/pyapi  gopytest/api 
```
while it does generate all the types and two python modules, the api and pyapi modules have circular import dependencies and can't be used properly. "api" module will contain functions that want to call into "pyapi" which has not been imported. And if it were imported manually its a breaking circular import.

(from pr-180)
> I'm pretty sure most of my current real-world use cases are slices of interface types and those all seem to be working properly without any bare handles being exposed.

Based on this suggestion, I tried adding a return type of a slice of interfaces, but it also ended up just creating a python list of handles.

title: Pass Go strings to Python as Unicode objects
body:
This PR builds on https://github.com/go-python/gopy/pull/170.

Currently, Python 3 CFFI returns Unicode, but Python 2 backends do not.

In Python 2, both byte and Unicode strings inherit from basestring and are interchangeable for most purposes. Some code might break, as Python 2 tries to encode Unicode strings to PYTHONIOENCODING while doing IO, and the specific encoding might be unable to represent the characters present in the string. Programs which ignore encoding _and_ have the need to output fancy characters suffer.

CPython 2 docstrings as Unicode are not implemented by this PR, but I do not think this is an issue.

Making the tests portable is difficult -- only the test for Unicode needs to handle IO encoding seriously. In the others I just strip the potential `u` which `repr` uses to mark Unicode strings in Python 2.

title: Support for pointer-to-slice parameters
body:
Implement support for pointer-to-slice parameters for methods and functions:

Python:
```
import tst
s = tst.StrSlice()
tst.Fill(s)
print s
```

Go:
```
package tst
type StrSlice []string
func Fill(s *StrSlice) {
        *s = StrSlice{"A", "B"}
}
```

This doesn't implement pointer support in general (as we didn't need it), but the fixes should make the implementation easier.

title: bind: support for cross-ref gc
body:
This CL implements a ref-counting scheme for go values passed to the python
world.
Go values (actually, their unsafe.Pointer) are associated with a reference
counter as soon as they exit the Go world.
These values are also pinned inside a global map to prevent the GC from moving
them around.
Conversely, python objects wrapping Go values will decrease the reference
counter when the **del** method is called.

Fixes #7

Change-Id: I9b6434d0933b7abe409ac130f3a8c0655c1f23a9

title: test(functional): fix Docker-backed SDK test infra
body:
Fixes #713

## Summary

Fixes the Docker-backed SDK functional test infrastructure and makes the new tests exercise real end-to-end behavior.

### Infrastructure fixes

- Use the repository root as the Docker build context so `deployments/docker/Dockerfile.control-plane` can copy `control-plane/...` paths.
- Poll the actual control-plane health route at `/api/v1/health` in Docker healthchecks and wait scripts.
- Use supported control-plane env vars for postgres mode: `AGENTFIELD_STORAGE_MODE=postgres` and `AGENTFIELD_POSTGRES_URL`.
- Add `host.docker.internal` host mapping so the containerized control plane can call agents running on the host during functional tests.

### SDK functional test fixes

- Update Python functional tests to use the current SDK API: `Agent(..., agentfield_server=...)`.
- Pass the `Agent` instance itself to uvicorn instead of `agent.app`.
- Start Python test agents on `0.0.0.0` while probing localhost for readiness.
- Avoid empty execute payloads rejected by the control plane.
- Add Go SDK skill registration support and include skills in control-plane node registration.
- Make Go agent startup bind its listener before registration so callback validation is not racy.
- Update Go functional execution coverage to actually call `/api/v1/execute/test-go-execution.add` and assert `sum == 5`.

## Verification

- `docker compose -f test-infra/docker-compose.yml config`
- `./test-infra/scripts/start-env.sh`
- `cd sdk/python && /tmp/agentfield-py-functional-venv/bin/pytest --collect-only -m functional tests/functional`
- `cd sdk/python && /tmp/agentfield-py-functional-venv/bin/pytest -m functional tests/functional/test_agent_registration.py -q`
- `cd sdk/go && go test ./agent`
- `cd sdk/go && go test -tags functional -run TestFunctionalExecution -count=1 -v .`

title: fix(harness): stream output with idle watchdog and explicit stdin null (#695)
body:
Fixes #695

## Problem

The CLI harness runner in all three SDKs blocked until the child process fully exited and read its output only at the end. Because it never streamed stdout, it could not apply a no-progress (idle) watchdog. When an opencode provider call stalled (commonly a stalled OpenRouter streaming response), the runner could not tell "stuck" from "working" and waited up to the wall-clock cap (default 1800s), freezing the run and holding a concurrency-semaphore slot the whole time. The TypeScript runner additionally opened stdin as a pipe that was never closed, so a child that probes stdin could hang waiting for EOF.

## Fix per language

Same shape in all three SDKs. Return types and full stdout/stderr strings are unchanged, so downstream JSONL parsing is unaffected. The wall-clock timeout is kept as the outer bound.

Python (`sdk/python/agentfield/harness/_cli.py`):
- Stream stdout and stderr concurrently into separate buffers, tracking the timestamp of the last received chunk.
- If no output arrives for `idle_seconds` (resolved from the `idle_seconds` arg, then env `AGENTFIELD_HARNESS_IDLE_SECONDS`, then 120s; a value <= 0 disables it), kill the process group and raise `TimeoutError`.
- Set `stdin=asyncio.subprocess.DEVNULL` and `start_new_session=True` so the child gets an immediate stdin EOF and its own process group.

Go (`sdk/go/harness/cli.go`, plus `cli_unix.go` and `cli_windows.go`):
- Replace `c.Run()` with in-memory buffers by `StdoutPipe`/`StderrPipe` drained in two goroutines, guarded by a mutex, recording last-activity.
- A one-second ticker checks the idle window and kills the process group when it is exceeded, returning a no-progress error.
- Set `c.Stdin` to an empty reader and run the child in its own process group (`Setpgid` on Unix, no-op on Windows).

TypeScript (`sdk/typescript/src/harness/cli.ts`):
- Add a `setInterval` idle watchdog over the existing concurrent data listeners; on idle it SIGKILLs the child and rejects with a no-progress error.
- Spawn with `stdio: ['ignore', 'pipe', 'pipe']` so the child's stdin receives an immediate EOF instead of an open pipe.
- Idle window resolves from the `idleSeconds` option, then env `AGENTFIELD_HARNESS_IDLE_SECONDS`, then 120s.

## Tests

Each SDK gains a test that spawns a child which prints one line then sleeps far past a short idle threshold (1s), asserting the runner aborts within seconds rather than at the wall-clock cap, plus a test that a normal fast command still returns its full output and exit code. Existing CLI tests that mocked the old wait-for-exit shape were updated to the streaming shape.

## Test plan

Python:

\`\`\`
cd sdk/python && uv run python -m pytest tests/test_run_cli_env.py tests/test_harness_cli.py tests/test_harness_runner.py tests/test_harness_provider_opencode.py tests/test_harness_provider_codex.py tests/test_harness_provider_gemini.py tests/test_harness_functional.py -q
# 86 passed
\`\`\`

Go:

\`\`\`
cd sdk/go && go build ./...        # ok
go vet ./harness/                  # no issues found
go test ./harness/ -run CLI -count=1   # ok, 7.5s
go test ./harness/ -count=1 -short     # 178 passed
\`\`\`

TypeScript:

\`\`\`
cd sdk/typescript && npx tsc --noEmit   # compilation completed
npm run build                           # ESM + DTS build success
npm test                                # 67 files, 633 tests passed
\`\`\`

The idle watchdog fires within roughly the configured window (about 1 to 2 seconds in the tests) instead of the 1800s wall-clock cap, and fast commands still return their complete output and exit code.
author: santoshkumarradha

linked_issues.title: Harness CLI runner waits for process exit without streaming or idle watchdog, causing 30-min stalls (Python, Go, TS)
linked_issues.body:
## Summary

The CLI-based harness runner blocks until the subprocess fully exits and reads its output only at the end. It never streams the child's stdout, so it cannot apply a no-progress (idle) watchdog. When a single `opencode` call stalls (most commonly a stalled OpenRouter streaming response), the harness cannot distinguish "stuck" from "working" and waits up to the full wall-clock cap (default 1800s = 30 min), freezing the run and holding a concurrency-semaphore slot the entire time, which starves every other in-flight experiment.

This is the same flaw in all three SDKs (Python, Go, TypeScript), and the TypeScript runner additionally mishandles stdin.

## Root cause

All three runners are "wait-for-exit, read-at-end":

- Python `sdk/python/agentfield/harness/_cli.py:33-44`: `await asyncio.wait_for(proc.communicate(), timeout=timeout)` returns only after EOF on both pipes (process exit). No streaming, no idle watchdog. `stdin` is not set, so the child inherits the parent's stdin.
- Go `sdk/go/harness/cli.go:76-80`: `c.Stdout = &stdout; c.Stderr = &stderr; err := c.Run()`. `c.Run()` is start + wait-for-exit into in-memory buffers. No streaming, no idle watchdog. `c.Stdin` is not set, so the child inherits the parent's stdin.
- TypeScript `sdk/typescript/src/harness/cli.ts:14-46`: resolves on `proc.on('close')` (wait-for-exit), accumulating stdout via `on('data')`. No completion detection from the stream. Worse, it spawns with `stdio: ['pipe', 'pipe', 'pipe']` and never writes to or closes `proc.stdin`, so the child's stdin is an open pipe that never receives EOF. If `opencode` reads stdin (CLIs commonly probe for piped input), it blocks forever, while Python and Go at least inherit `/dev/null` and get an immediate EOF.

The only safety net is the coarse wall-clock timeout (`AGENTFIELD_HARNESS_TIMEOUT_SECONDS`, default 1800s). Raising or lowering it is not a fix: too high freezes the run for up to 30 minutes per stall; too low kills legitimately long experiments

title: Add workflow restart and replay reuse
body:
## Summary
- add a control-plane restart endpoint for executions with workflow/node scope, replay reuse, fork metadata, and run lineage
- expose restart from CLI, Python SDK, TypeScript SDK, run detail, DAG node sidebar, and run lifecycle menus
- surface replay, golden, and fork/restart lineage in run and DAG APIs without adding user-authored replay APIs

## Product design
- v1 gives failed workflow recovery: restart from the failed run or from a specific node, reusing already-successful `app.call` outputs where the control plane has persisted inputs/outputs
- v1.5 opens comparison and governance workflows without extra product surface: fork with changed input/model, mark a good run golden, and inspect which nodes were reused vs rerun
- default recovery is one-click **Restart run** from the run detail header or row menu; advanced paths stay behind existing overflow/menu patterns
- **Fork with changes** is intentional branching, not a separate dashboard; it uses the same restart primitive with changed input/reuse settings
- run detail and run list show small `Golden` plus `Restarted`/`Forked` metadata chips so users can see lineage without another table column
- reused nodes are marked only where the user scans execution behavior: compact branch markers in trace rows and graph nodes, plus one source execution line in the graph node sidebar
- new UI accents use existing `statusTone` theme tokens; no standalone color palette was added

## Docs PR
Website docs/screenshots are in https://github.com/Agent-Field/website2.0/pull/27. Merge that after this platform PR so the public docs only describe released API/UI behavior.

## Screenshots
![Golden restarted run detail](https://github.com/Agent-Field/agentfield/blob/codex/workflow-restart-fork-replay/docs/design/screenshots/workflow-restart-golden-detail.png?raw=1)
[Open full size](https://github.com/Agent-Field/agentfield/blob/codex/workflow-restart-fork-replay/docs/design/screenshots/workflow-restart-golden-detail.png)

![Golden-only runs list with lineage chip](https://github.com/Agent-Field/agentfield/blob/codex/workflow-restart-fork-replay/docs/design/screenshots/workflow-restart-golden-runs-list.png?raw=1)
[Open full size](https://github.com/Agent-Field/agentfield/blob/codex/workflow-restart-fork-replay/docs/design/screenshots/workflow-restart-golden-runs-list.png)

![DAG graph for restarted run](https://github.com/Agent-Field/agentfield/blob/codex/workflow-restart-fork-replay/docs/design/screenshots/workflow-restart-graph-reuse.png?raw=1)
[Open full size](https://github.com/Agent-Field/agentfield/blob/codex/workflow-restart-fork-replay/docs/design/screenshots/workflow-restart-graph-reuse.png)

![Reused node sidebar provenance](https://github.com/Agent-Field/agentfield/blob/codex/workflow-restart-fork-replay/docs/design/screenshots/workflow-restart-reuse-sidebar.png?raw=1)
[Open full size](https://github.com/Agent-Field/agentfield/blob/codex/workflow-restart-fork-replay/docs/design/screenshots/workflow-restart-reuse-sidebar.png)

![Restart actions menu](https://github.com/Agent-Field/agentfield/blob/codex/workflow-restart-fork-replay/docs/design/screenshots/workflow-restart-actions-menu.png?raw=1)
[Open full size](https://github.com/Agent-Field/agentfield/blob/codex/workflow-restart-fork-replay/docs/design/screenshots/workflow-restart-actions-menu.png)

## Validation
- `cd control-plane && go test ./internal/handlers ./internal/handlers/ui ./internal/server ./internal/cli`
- `cd control-plane && go test ./internal/handlers -run 'TestExecute.*Replay|TestRestartExecutionHandler|TestExecutionReuseInfo'`
- `cd control-plane/web/client && npm test -- --run src/components/runs/RunLifecycleMenu.test.tsx src/test/pages/RunsPage.test.tsx src/test/pages/RunDetailPage.test.tsx src/test/components/WorkflowDAG/nodeDetailSidebar.test.tsx src/test/components/RunTrace.test.tsx` -> 41 passed
- `cd control-plane/web/client && npm run build`
- `./scripts/coverage-surface.sh web-ui` -> 128 files / 665 tests passed
- `cd sdk/typescript && npm run lint && npm run build`
- `python3 -m py_compile sdk/python/agentfield/async_execution_manager.py sdk/python/tests/test_async_execution_manager_final90.py tests/functional/agents/restart_replay_agent.py tests/functional/tests/test_restart_replay.py`
- `cd sdk/python && python3 -m pytest tests/test_async_execution_manager_final90.py::test_update_execution_from_status_populates_same_status_success tests/test_execution_context_core.py -q` -> 11 passed
- Docker restart/replay E2E: `OPENROUTER_MODEL=openrouter/google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite PYTEST_ARGS="-v -n 1 tests/test_restart_replay.py::test_restart_reuses_successful_calls_and_continues_complex_openrouter_graph" docker compose -f docker/docker-compose.local.yml -f /tmp/agentfield-compose-no-host-ports.yml up --build --abort-on-container-exit --exit-code-from test-runner` -> 1 passed in 13.41s
- OpenRouter provider smoke: `google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite` chat completions request with local `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` -> 200 `ok`

Note: the restart/replay E2E graph uses deterministic checkpoints so replay correctness is not coupled to provider latency; the separate OpenRouter smoke verifies the requested model/key path.
author: santoshkumarradha

title: Add ARD discovery workspace
body:
## Screenshots

![ARD Discovery UX screenshots](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Agent-Field/agentfield/codex/ard-discovery-e2e/docs/assets/pr-675-ard-discovery/ard-discovery-contact-sheet.png)

## Summary

This PR adds first-class [Agentic Resource Discovery (ARD)](https://agenticresourcediscovery.org/) support to the AgentField control plane as an explicit discovery workflow, not a passive global switch.

References used for the implementation shape:
- [ARD publishing guide](https://agenticresourcediscovery.org/how_to_publish/#step-1-create-the-manifest-ai-catalogjson)
- [ARD specification](https://agenticresourcediscovery.org/spec/)
- [ards-project/ard-spec](https://github.com/ards-project/ard-spec)

It introduces:
- ARD config guardrails and runtime state storage.
- Public catalog serving at `/.well-known/ai-catalog.json`.
- ARD artifact, registry search, registry explore, and registry agent-list endpoints.
- A new top-level **Discovery** workspace in the embedded UI.
- Per-reasoner / per-skill opt-in publishing controls.
- External ARD search, import, and explicit callability bindings.

## Architecture
The implementation separates deployment safety boundaries from runtime operator intent.

Config/env decides what is allowed:
- `ard.enabled`
- `public_base_url`
- `publisher_domain`
- `publish.enabled`
- `registry.enabled`
- `registry.public`
- `external.search_enabled`
- `external.invocation_enabled`
- `external.allowed_registries`

Database-backed runtime state decides what is currently enabled:
- global ARD UI settings where config does not lock the field
- per reasoner/skill exposure toggles
- publication metadata overrides
- validation status
- imported external entries
- callable bindings and adapter settings
- registry records

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  subgraph Config["Config / env safety boundaries"]
    C1["ARD enabled"]
    C2["Publish allowed"]
    C3["Public base URL"]
    C4["Publisher domain"]
    C5["External search allowed"]
    C6["External invocation allowed"]
    C7["Allowed registries"]
  end

  subgraph DB["Database runtime state"]
    D1["Runtime UI settings"]
    D2["Per-reasoner exposure"]
    D3["Metadata overrides"]
    D4["Imported entries"]
    D5["Callable bindings"]
    D6["Registry records"]
  end

  subgraph ControlPlane["AgentField control plane"]
    A1["ARD effective config"]
    A2["Catalog builder"]
    A3["Public ARD routes"]
    A4["Discovery UI"]
  end

  Config --> A1
  DB --> A1
  A1 --> A2
  A2 --> A3
  A4 --> DB
```

The key rule is preserved throughout the stack:
- `ARD enabled` means the feature is available.
- `Publish catalog` means public routes may serve entries.
- `Expose through ARD` means a specific reasoner or skill is public.
- `Import` means AgentField knows an external ARD resource exists.
- `Make callable` means SDK routing may call it.

Global enablement never publishes internal capabilities or invokes external resources by itself.

## Backend Design
New ARD domain logic builds a schema-clean catalog from AgentField nodes, reasoners, and skills. Runtime metadata is projected into ARD entries, while AgentField-specific invocation metadata stays on the internal publication view and generated artifacts.

Public and registry endpoints:
- `GET /.well-known/ai-catalog.json`
- `GET /api/v1/ard/artifacts/:entryID`
- `POST /api/v1/ard/search`
- `GET /api/v1/ard/agents`
- `POST /api/v1/ard/explore`

UI/admin endpoints manage runtime state:
- Discovery dashboard
- settings updates
- publication saves
- external search
- external imports
- callable bindings
- registry records

External search is allowlist-based and blocks cross-host redirects. External invocation remains impossible until both config allows it and the imported entry has an explicit callable binding.

## UX Flow
ARD is placed where users will actually operate discovery:
- Sidebar: new **Discovery** top-level area.
- Discovery tabs: `Overview`, `Public Catalog`, `External Search`, `Imports`, `Registry`.
- Settings -> Discovery: deployment/config status, locked fields, DID/trust, auth/CORS/public URL readiness.
- Agents page: ARD badges, publish affordances, exposure editing links.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  actor Admin
  actor Developer
  participant UI as Discovery UI
  participant CP as Control Plane
  participant Catalog as /.well-known/ai-catalog.json
  participant Registry as External ARD Registry
  participant SDK as AgentField SDK

  Admin->>CP: Configure public_base_url, publisher_domain, ARD gates
  Developer->>UI: Open Discovery Overview
  UI->>CP: Read effective ARD setup and warnings
  Developer->>UI: Toggle Expose through ARD for a reasoner
  UI->>CP: Save publication metadata and validation state
  CP->>Catalog: Serve only published valid entries
  Developer->>UI: Search allowed external registries
  UI->>Registry: ARD search / explore
  Developer->>UI: Import selected external entry
  UI->>CP: Store imported ARD entry
  Developer->>UI: Enable Make callable and configure adapter
  UI->>CP: Store callable binding
  SDK->>CP: app.call("external.vendor.review_contract", input)
```

The intended operator flow is:
1. Admin configures public URL, publisher domain, and ARD feature gates.
2. Operator opens `Discovery -> Overview` and fixes setup warnings.
3. Developer exposes a specific reasoner or skill through ARD.
4. UI validates required metadata before publication.
5. Published entries appear in `Discovery -> Public Catalog` and the public catalog route.
6. Developer searches allowed external registries.
7. Selected external entries are imported.
8. Only imported entries with `Make callable` enabled can become SDK targets such as `external.vendor.review_contract`.

## DX
The developer-facing model stays explicit and auditable.

Internal publishing:
```python
# External clients discover AgentField capabilities through:
# https://control-plane.example.com/.well-known/ai-catalog.json
```

External callability after import and binding:
```python
result = await app.call("external.vendor.review_contract", input)
```

This PR does not make arbitrary external ARD entries callable by default. The local target name, adapter, auth reference, timeout, allowed operations, and policy are runtime binding state.

## TDD / Validation Shape
This PR is being treated as a test-driven integration, because the risky parts are policy boundaries rather than UI rendering alone.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  T1["Config gate tests"] --> T2["Runtime state tests"]
  T2 --> T3["Catalog manifest tests"]
  T3 --> T4["Public route tests"]
  T4 --> T5["Registry protocol tests"]
  T5 --> T6["UI workflow tests"]
  T6 --> T7["Patch coverage gate"]

  T1 -. protects .-> R1["Global enablement does not publish"]
  T2 -. protects .-> R2["Database opt-in is explicit"]
  T3 -. protects .-> R3["Schema-clean ai-catalog.json"]
  T4 -. protects .-> R4["Public routes honor publish gates"]
  T5 -. protects .-> R5["External registries are allowlisted"]
  T6 -. protects .-> R6["Import is separate from callability"]
```

Coverage targets added or expanded around:
- ARD config/env parsing and locked effective settings.
- State load/save defaults and error paths.
- Catalog generation and artifact generation.
- Public catalog and registry route behavior.
- Registry search/explore request shapes.
- External search allowlist and redirect safety.
- UI Discovery tabs, publication editing, imports, callable binding, registry editing, and API client behavior.

## Registry / External Agents
External agents can be discovered by AgentField through configured ARD registries. AgentField can also expose its own catalog for external crawlers or registries.

Registration is intentionally adapter-aware:
- If a registry supports submission, the Registry tab can track submission state.
- If not, AgentField exposes the catalog URL for manual submission or crawling.

This keeps the first version compatible with the ARD discovery model without overpromising a universal cross-agent invocation protocol.

## Verification
Completed locally during PR development:
- focused Go tests for ARD domain, handlers, server route wiring, config, and auth middleware
- focused frontend tests for Discovery, Agents, Settings, App shell, and ARD API client
- frontend production build
- web UI coverage surface
- control-plane coverage surface with local Python 3.12 shim

Current draft status:
- The remaining active gate is patch coverage for newly touched control-plane lines.
- Follow-up test-only coverage work is in progress before marking this ready.
author: santoshkumarradha

title: Add Databricks integration pack
body:
## Summary

Adds the first-party Databricks integration pack:

- built-in `databricks` control-plane source for Databricks notification-destination webhooks
- Docker-hostable Go Databricks node that exposes Databricks as callable AgentField capabilities
- Python example node that consumes the Databricks node through `app.call`, rather than embedding its own Databricks client
- UI metadata/defaults for creating Databricks triggers from the integrations and trigger setup flows
- trigger dispatch lifecycle records so webhook-to-node runs have execution/workflow records before async callbacks arrive

The integration keeps the intended AgentField boundary: the control plane owns provider triggers, and the optional provider node owns provider-native capabilities. Other nodes call the Databricks node by capability ID.

This PR intentionally keeps documentation local to `integrations/databricks/`. Public docs-site content belongs in the separate website repo.

## Developer Experience

1. Create a Databricks trigger in the control-plane UI/API.
   - Source: `databricks`
   - Target: any webhook-enabled reasoner, for example `databricks-prod.handle_databricks_event` or `databricks-incident-reviewer.review_databricks_event`
   - Secret env var: `DATABRICKS_TRIGGER_SECRET`
   - Config supports basic or bearer verification and JSON paths for event type/id/workspace.

2. Deploy the Databricks node when users want Databricks capabilities available to other nodes.

```bash
docker build -f integrations/databricks/node/Dockerfile -t agentfield/databricks-node .
docker run --rm \
  -e AGENTFIELD_URL=http://host.docker.internal:18080 \
  -e AGENT_NODE_ID=databricks-prod \
  -e AGENT_CALLBACK_URL=http://localhost:18112 \
  -e DATABRICKS_HOST=https://dbc-xxx.cloud.databricks.com \
  -e DATABRICKS_TOKEN=... \
  -e DATABRICKS_WAREHOUSE_ID=... \
  -p 18112:18112 \
  agentfield/databricks-node
```

3. Other nodes use it as a source of Databricks-native functions and AI calls:

```python
metrics = await app.call(
    "databricks-prod.query_readonly",
    sql="SELECT event_type, COUNT(*) FROM main.ops.events GROUP BY event_type",
)

review = await app.call(
    "databricks-prod.ai_query",
    endpoint="databricks-meta-llama-3-1-8b-instruct",
    prompt="Summarize the operational risk in this result set as compact JSON.",
    fail_on_error=True,
)
```

## Databricks Capabilities

The Go node registers:

- `health`
- `query_readonly`
- `describe_table`
- `search_columns`
- `ai_query`
- `invoke_serving_endpoint`
- `explain_result`
- `investigate_metric_change`
- `handle_databricks_event`

Databricks-native AI remains Databricks-native:

- `ai_query` runs through Databricks SQL Warehouses.
- `invoke_serving_endpoint` calls Databricks Model Serving.
- Prompt-backed helpers load configurable templates from `DATABRICKS_PROMPTS_FILE` or packaged defaults.

## Example

The useful example lives in the integration pack:

- `integrations/databricks/examples/incident-reviewer/main.py`

It is a separate Python AgentField node that receives a Databricks trigger event and calls:

- `databricks-prod.query_readonly`
- `databricks-prod.ai_query`
- `databricks-prod.investigate_metric_change`

## Live E2E Evidence

Using Databricks CLI profile `af-databricks-e2e` against workspace `https://dbc-f0aa20f5-85c2.cloud.databricks.com`:

- SQL warehouse: `9ebaa0cbec4347d6` (`Serverless Starter Warehouse`)
- Test table: `workspace.agentfield_e2e.agentfield_e2e_events`
- Query through Databricks node succeeded: `exec_20260615_105719_0rew6vmm`
- Databricks trigger to node succeeded after dispatcher lifecycle fix:
  - inbound trigger execution: `exec_20260615_105823_x9isuw0y`
  - workflow: `wf_20260615_105823_dinr485b`
  - node execution: `exec_20260615_105823_6ge710of`
- Model Serving invocation succeeded against `databricks-meta-llama-3-1-8b-instruct`: `exec_20260615_110030_wxybd498`
- SQL `ai_query` succeeded: `exec_20260615_110043_axjtkj8i`
- Warehouse was stopped after E2E validation.

No provider tokens or OpenRouter secrets are included in this PR.

## Validation

- `python3 -m py_compile integrations/databricks/examples/incident-reviewer/main.py`
- `go test ./internal/sources/databricks ./internal/sources/all ./internal/services -run 'TestDatabricks|TestAll|TestTriggerDispatcher'`
- `go test ./...` from `integrations/databricks/node`
- `npm run build` from `control-plane/web/client`
- `docker build -f integrations/databricks/node/Dockerfile -t agentfield/databricks-node:e2e .`
- `npm test -- --run src/components/triggers/databricks-trigger-ui.test.tsx`
- `./scripts/coverage-surface.sh web-ui && ./scripts/patch-coverage-gate.sh` (web-ui patch coverage: 100.00% on 3 touched lines)

Note: a full local `./scripts/coverage-summary.sh && ./scripts/patch-coverage-gate.sh` run stopped in the pre-existing Go server coverage test `TestStartAndStopCoverAdditionalBranches` before the patch gate could run all surfaces. The failing PR surface from CI (`web-ui`) is now verified independently with the repo surface script and patch gate.

## Reference Docs Checked

- Databricks SQL Statement Execution API: https://docs.databricks.com/api/workspace/statementexecution
- Databricks notification destinations: https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/admin/workspace-settings/notification-destinations
- Databricks AI Functions: https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/large-language-models/ai-functions
- Databricks `ai_query`: https://docs.databricks.com/aws/en/sql/language-manual/functions/ai_query
- Databricks Serving Endpoint query API: https://docs.databricks.com/api/workspace/servingendpoints/query

title: Add AgentField realtime sessions for WebRTC voice ingress
body:
## Summary

This PR introduces the AgentField session DX for realtime voice and multimodal ingress through the control plane.

The product goal is that browsers, CLIs, and external clients do not connect directly to model providers. They create an AgentField session, and the control plane owns the provider boundary, realtime transport, tool calls, and workflow provenance.

## What Changed

- Adds Python `@app.session(...)` for declaring long-lived realtime/multimodal session handlers.
- Adds Python `RealtimeSession`, `SessionTurn`, `session.input()`, `session.say(...)`, and `session.call(...)` as the handler-facing DX.
- Adds TypeScript `agent.session(...)` and Go `RegisterSession(...)` equivalents so session declarations register consistently across SDKs.
- Adds explicit provider + transport validation in Python, TypeScript, Go, and the control plane.
- Adds control-plane session routes:
  - `POST /api/v1/sessions/:target/start`
  - `POST /api/v1/sessions/:session_id/realtime-offer`
  - `POST /api/v1/sessions/:session_id/tools/:tool`
- Adds OpenAI WebRTC SDP offer proxying through the control plane for `provider=openai`, `transport=webrtc`.
- Adds `af session` CLI commands for start, SDP offer exchange, tool invocation, and workflow lookup.
- Keeps session tool work on AgentField `execute/async`, forwarding `X-Session-ID` so resulting reasoner work belongs to the session and appears in the normal workflow DAG.
- Fixes Python nested `app.call` parent headers by forwarding the current execution as `X-Parent-Execution-ID`.
- Adds a TODO beside control-plane session relationship fields for future explicit session-to-session lifecycle edges.

## DX Shape

```python
@app.session(
    "voice",
    provider="openai",
    model="gpt-realtime-2",
    transport="webrtc",
    modalities=["audio", "text"],
    voice="marin",
    tools=["support.resolve_voice_turn"],
)
async def voice(session):
    turn = await session.input()
    result = await session.call("support.resolve_voice_turn", turn=turn)
    await session.say(result["spoken_response"])
```

Provider and transport are explicit controls:

- `provider` selects who runs the realtime/audio model.
- `transport` selects how the session moves audio/events through the control plane.
- AgentField validates the pair, but does not infer a transport or silently switch providers.

Example validation behavior:

```text
Unsupported session transport 'webrtc' for provider 'openrouter'. Supported transports: audio_turns. AgentField does not infer or switch providers; set provider and transport explicitly.
```

## CLI Shape

```bash
af session start voice-support-af.voice \
  --provider openai \
  --transport webrtc \
  --model gpt-realtime-2 \
  --voice marin

# Pipe-friendly SDP exchange through the control plane.
af session offer sess_123 \
  --provider openai \
  --transport webrtc \
  --sdp @offer.sdp > answer.sdp

# Session tools still route through AgentField execute/async.
af session tool sess_123 launch_support_workflow \
  --target voice-support-af.resolve_voice_turn \
  --in '{"utterance":"I need help with a delayed order"}'

af session workflows sess_123
```

`af session offer` accepts inline SDP, `--sdp @path`, or stdin. The default output is raw SDP so browser automation and WebRTC tooling can pipe it directly; `--output json` wraps the answer as `{ "answer_sdp": "..." }`.

## Product Positioning

Sessions make AgentField the ingress layer for realtime AI applications.

A browser voice call is not a side channel around AgentField. It becomes an AgentField session backed by the control plane. Browser WebRTC setup, provider negotiation, and tool calls all go through AgentField, while every agent action still routes through the existing execution API.

That gives realtime voice apps the same properties as normal AgentField workflows:

- provider boundary owned by the control plane
- explicit provider and transport configuration
- session-scoped execution context
- reasoner-to-reasoner workflow DAGs
- replay/provenance surfaces for agent work
- consistent SDK shape across Python, TypeScript, and Go

## Docs Follow-up

Website docs PR: Agent-Field/website2.0#28.

Merge this AgentField runtime/SDK/control-plane PR first. Merge the website docs PR after this lands so the public docs do not describe session APIs before they are available.

## Validation

- `uv run pytest tests/test_agent_session.py tests/test_session_transport.py tests/test_execution_context_core.py -q`: 13 passed.
- `npm test` from `sdk/typescript`: 619 passed.
- `npm run lint`: passed.
- `go test ./agent -run 'TestValidateSessionTransport|TestAgentRegisterSession|TestAgent'` from `sdk/go`: passed.
- `go test ./pkg/types ./internal/handlers ./internal/cli` from `control-plane`: passed.
- Earlier live voice spike validated browser WebRTC ingress through an AgentField-style session route into OpenAI Realtime, with session tool calls launching a control-plane workflow and preserving a 9-node AgentField DAG.

## Session Tool Semantics

`session.call(...)` and `tools=[...]` serve different paths:

- `session.call(...)` is handler-controlled orchestration. The session function can call AgentField reasoners or skills directly without listing them in `tools`.
- `tools=[...]` is the provider/client-visible allowlist for autonomous realtime tool calls during the live session. For example, a realtime audio model or browser-side tool bridge can choose to call `orders.lookup_order`; AgentField then routes that request through `/api/v1/sessions/:session_id/tools/:tool` into `execute/async` with the session ID attached.

So `tools` is not Python dependency injection and not required for normal handler code. It is the explicit exposure boundary for capabilities the live audio loop may invoke.
author: santoshkumarradha

title: Fix: Remove /api/ui/v1 URL substitution from Python & Go SDK note endpoints
body:
## Summary
- Removed the `/api/ui/v1` URL substitution from both Python SDK and Go SDK `note()` implementations so notes are sent directly to the canonical `/api/v1/executions/note` endpoint
- Removed tracked build artifacts (`.DS_Store`, coverage files, binaries) and fortified `.gitignore` to prevent re-introduction
- Updated Go SDK `note_test.go` assertions to reflect the corrected URL behavior in both the basic and URL-conversion test cases

## Changes
- `sdk/python/agentfield/agent.py` — Stripped `/api/ui/v1` URL replacement in both `aiohttp` and `requests` code paths; consolidated debug logging
- `sdk/go/agent/note.go` — Removed `/api/ui/v1` URL construction logic; notes now use the base URL directly
- `sdk/go/agent/note_test.go` — Updated `expectedPath` values to match new canonical URL
- `.gitignore` — Added entries for `.DS_Store`, coverage files, binaries, and other build artifacts
- Various `.DS_Store`, `coverage-*.txt`, `coverage_*.out`, `sdk/py_cov.txt`, `sdk/ts_cov.txt` — Removed from tracking

## Test plan
1. `cd sdk/go && go test ./agent/ -run TestNote` — Go SDK note tests pass with corrected URL expectations
2. `cd sdk/python && pytest tests/test_agent_core.py` — Python SDK tests pass (including note-related assertions)
3. Verify no tracked build artifacts remain: `git ls-files '*.DS_Store' 'coverage-*' 'coverage_*'` should be empty

🤖 Built with [AgentField SWE-AF](https://github.com/Agent-Field/SWE-AF)
🔌 Powered by [AgentField](https://github.com/Agent-Field/agentfield)
author: AbirAbbas

title: Generic strong-type the consumer-facing API in Python SDK
body:
## Summary

- Added TypeVar `T`, `R` and ParamSpec `P` to core decorators (`reasoner`, `skill`, `on_event`, `on_schedule`, `on_change`) so decorated functions preserve their original return types instead of returning `Any`/`Unknown`
- Made `Agent.reasoner()`, `Agent.skill()`, and module-level decorator wrappers generic over their invocation parameters and return types, improving IDE autocompletion and mypy accuracy
- Introduced a `RouteRegistrar` interface in the Go control plane using `gin.IRouter` to enable polymorphic handler composition and easier testing with mocks
- Added mypy type-inference test suite covering sync/async reasoners, event handlers, and `simulate_trigger`/`simulate_schedule` with generic `R` for end-to-end type safety

## Changes

**Python SDK**
- `sdk/python/agentfield/decorators.py` — Generic overloads and ParamSpec/TypeVar on `reasoner`, `on_event`, `on_schedule`, `on_change`
- `sdk/python/agentfield/agent.py` — Generic type preservation on `Agent.reasoner()`, `skill()`, `on_change()`
- `sdk/python/agentfield/router.py` — Indentation fix for nested `decorator()` functions; corrected `on_change` type to `Callable[P, Awaitable[T]]`
- `sdk/python/agentfield/testing.py` — `TypeVar R` on `simulate_trigger`/`simulate_schedule` for type-safe assertions
- `sdk/python/agentfield/node_logs.py` — Minor typing improvements
- `sdk/python/mypy.ini` — mypy configuration for strict type checking
- `sdk/python/tests/` — New test files for mypy type inference and generic return type propagation

**Go Control Plane**
- `control-plane/internal/handlers/route_registrar.go` — New `RouteRegistrar` interface using `gin.IRouter`
- `control-plane/internal/handlers/connector/handlers.go`, `config_storage.go`, `did_handlers.go`, `admin/` — Updated all RegisterRoutes signatures to use `gin.IRouter`

## Test plan

1. `cd sdk/python && python -m pytest tests/` — All 15+ existing and new tests pass
2. `cd sdk/python && mypy agentfield/` — No new mypy errors; decorated functions correctly infer return types
3. `cd sdk/python && mypy tests/mypy_reveal_simulate_trigger.py tests/mypy_reveal_simulate_schedule.py` — Revealed types match expected generic return types
4. Go control-plane builds and unit tests continue to pass with the `gin.IRouter` refactor

🤖 Built with [AgentField SWE-AF](https://github.com/Agent-Field/SWE-AF)
🔌 Powered by [AgentField](https://github.com/Agent-Field/agentfield)
author: AbirAbbas

title: Fix multimedia OpenRouter/Fal SDK DX
body:
## Summary
- Fix OpenRouter media provider defaults to live image/TTS models while keeping routing/provider APIs unchanged.
- Parse OpenRouter image outputs directly from chat completions so Gemini/Grok `message.images[]` responses work reliably.
- Add video input helpers across Python, TypeScript, and Go, matching the existing image/audio helper pattern.
- Avoid LiteLLM trimming on untrimmable media payloads to prevent base64 logging/failures.
- Fix Fal audio response metadata so model-specific `output_format` is reflected in `AudioOutput.format`.

## Live verification
OpenRouter:
- Python image/audio/video input and image/audio/video generation passed.
- TypeScript image/audio/video generation passed.
- Go image/audio/video input and image/audio/video generation passed.

Fal:
- Python Fal image/audio/video generation passed.

## Tests
- `uv run --project sdk/python pytest sdk/python/tests/test_multimodal.py sdk/python/tests/test_media_providers.py sdk/python/tests/test_openrouter_audio.py sdk/python/tests/test_openrouter_video.py sdk/python/tests/test_media_integration.py sdk/python/tests/test_vision.py sdk/python/tests/test_image_config.py`

title: feat(cli): add minimal trigger surface
body:
Closes #576

## Summary
- Add root-level `af call`, `af ls`, and `af tail` commands for the minimal human trigger surface.
- Add reasoner catalog and execution event stream API routes used by the CLI.
- Add focused CLI/handler unit tests and a functional Docker e2e covering call/schema/ls/async/tail.

- `go test ./cmd/af ./internal/cli ./internal/handlers ./internal/server -run 'Test(NewRoot|ResolveCall|ValidateInput|ExitCode|ListReasoners|StreamExecution|SetupRoutes|StartAdmin)'`\n- `go test ./cmd/af ./internal/cli ./internal/handlers`\n- `go build -tags "sqlite_fts5" -o /tmp/af-issue-576 ./cmd/af`\n- `python3 -m py_compile tests/functional/tests/test_af_trigger_cli.py`\n- `OPENROUTER_API_KEY=dummy PYTEST_ARGS='-q tests/test_af_trigger_cli.py' docker compose -f docker/docker-compose.local.yml -f <(printf 'services:\\n  control-plane:\\n    ports: !reset []\\n') up --build --abort-on-container-exit --exit-code-from test-runner`\n\nNote: the functional Docker run used a transient compose override to remove the host `8080:8080` publish because port 8080 was already in use locally; service-to-service traffic still used `control-plane:8080` inside Docker.
